name: Confluent Certified Developer for Apache Kafka

questions.q: A topic has 6 partitions. Which ordering guarantee does Kafka provide for its records?
options: Total order is maintained across the whole topic regardless of partition
Order is guaranteed only within each partition
Ordering is guaranteed per broker across all of its partitions
No ordering is guaranteed once a topic has multiple partitions
questions.explanation: Each partition is an ordered append-only log, but there is no coordination of order ACROSS partitions. Records that must be ordered relative to each other must share a partition — which is why they are given the same key.
optionNotes: Kafka provides no global cross-partition ordering.
Correct — records are strictly ordered by offset within each partition, not across the topic.
Ordering is per partition, not per broker; a broker hosts many partitions.
There is a guarantee — within a single partition records are strictly ordered.

questions.q: All events for a given customer must be processed in order. How is this achieved when producing?
options: Set acks=all so every write is fully replicated before ack
Use the customer ID as the record key so a customer's events hash to one partition
Use a single-partition topic so all customer events share one log
Enable compression to keep related events batched together
questions.explanation: Keyed records are partitioned by murmur2(key) % numPartitions, so one customer's events always land on the same partition and stay ordered. A single partition would also work but destroys parallelism for all customers.
optionNotes: acks governs durability, not ordering or which partition a record lands in.
Correct — keying by customer ID routes all of that customer's events to the same partition, preserving order.
A single partition preserves order but destroys parallelism; keying is the scalable answer.
Compression affects size, not partition routing.

questions.q: A consumer group with 8 consumers reads a topic with 6 partitions. What happens?
options: Two consumers remain idle with no partitions assigned
All 8 consumers share the partitions round-robin per record
The consumer group fails to start with too many members
Kafka automatically adds 2 partitions to balance the group
questions.explanation: Within a group each partition goes to exactly one consumer, so at most 6 consumers can be active on 6 partitions — the extra 2 get nothing. Partition count is the group's maximum parallelism.
optionNotes: Correct — a partition goes to at most one consumer per group, so with 6 partitions two of the 8 consumers stay idle.
Partitions are not split per record across consumers; each partition has one owner in the group.
Extra consumers are allowed; they simply get no assignment.
Kafka never auto-adds partitions to match consumer count.

questions.q: A topic is configured with replication.factor=3 and min.insync.replicas=2, and producers use acks=all. What does this setup guarantee?
options: Writes still succeed even if all three replicas are unavailable
Every acknowledged write is on at least 2 replicas, surviving one broker failure
Exactly-once delivery of each record to consumers
Consumers may read from follower replicas for lower latency
questions.explanation: acks=all waits for all in-sync replicas, and min.insync.replicas=2 refuses writes if fewer than 2 are in sync. One broker can fail (ISR=2 still meets the minimum); acknowledged data always exists on ≥2 replicas. This is the canonical durability configuration.
optionNotes: With acks=all and min.insync=2, writes fail if fewer than 2 replicas are in sync.
Correct — acks=all + min.insync.replicas=2 means acked data lives on 2+ replicas and tolerates one broker loss.
This is a durability guarantee, not exactly-once consumer delivery.
By default consumers read only from the leader, not followers.

questions.q: With min.insync.replicas=2 on an RF=3 topic, two brokers holding replicas of a partition go down. What do producers using acks=all experience?
options: Writes continue normally against the single remaining in-sync replica
Writes fail with NotEnoughReplicas errors until the ISR recovers
Data is silently redirected to a different topic partition
Consumers also immediately stop being able to read the partition
questions.explanation: The ISR (1) is below min.insync.replicas (2), so acks=all produces are rejected — availability is sacrificed to protect durability. Reads of already-committed data can continue from the remaining leader.
optionNotes: min.insync.replicas=2 forbids acking against just one replica.
Correct — with only one in-sync replica, acks=all writes are rejected (NotEnoughReplicas) until the ISR recovers.
Kafka never silently reroutes data to another topic.
Reads can continue from the leader; it is writes that are blocked.

questions.q: Which statement describes log compaction (cleanup.policy=compact)?
options: Records older than retention.ms are deleted regardless of their key or value
Kafka retains at least the latest value per key; a null value (tombstone) deletes a key
The log segments are transparently compressed with zstd
Only records that consumers have already read are removed
questions.explanation: Compaction is per-key retention of the latest value — turning a topic into a durable changelog (great for table-like state). Tombstones delete keys. Time/size deletion is the 'delete' policy; consumption never removes data.
optionNotes: That describes delete retention, not compaction.
Correct — compaction keeps the most recent value per key; a null-value tombstone marks a key for removal.
Compaction is about keys, not byte-level compression.
Compaction is independent of what consumers have read.

questions.q: Two different applications, each with its own group.id, consume the same topic. How is data delivered?
options: The two applications split the topic's records between them
Each group receives ALL records; within a group, partitions divide among its consumers
Only the first group to subscribe receives the topic's data
Both groups must be configured with an identical number of consumers to work
questions.explanation: Consumer groups are independent — Kafka is pub-sub across groups and queue-like within a group. Each group maintains its own offsets in __consumer_offsets.
optionNotes: Different group.ids do not share records; each group gets the full stream.
Correct — every consumer group independently receives all records; partitions are split only within a group.
All subscribed groups receive data, not just the first.
Groups are independent; their consumer counts need not match.

questions.q: Which combination of client settings secures a connection to a SASL/SCRAM-secured cluster over TLS?
options: security.protocol=PLAINTEXT together with a topic ACL to secure the client connection to the SCRAM cluster
security.protocol=SASL_SSL, sasl.mechanism=SCRAM-SHA-512, JAAS credentials, and a broker truststore
compression.type=ssl to encrypt the payloads
enable.idempotence=true to secure the producer writes
questions.explanation: SASL_SSL = SASL authentication over a TLS-encrypted channel; the mechanism and JAAS config carry the SCRAM credentials, and the truststore validates the brokers. ACLs then authorize the authenticated principal.
optionNotes: PLAINTEXT gives no TLS; ACLs are authorization, not the transport/auth mechanism.
Correct — SASL_SSL + SCRAM mechanism + JAAS credentials + a truststore is the standard secured-client config.
There is no 'ssl' compression codec; compression is not security.
Idempotence prevents duplicate writes; it does nothing for authentication or encryption.

questions.q: A consumer application is denied with a TopicAuthorizationException although the topic exists. What is the most likely cause?
options: The topic has far too many partitions for the consumer
The client's principal lacks the required ACLs (Read on the topic and on the group)
The consumer is simply configured with the wrong compression codec for that particular topic
The consumer left auto.offset.reset unset
questions.explanation: Authorization errors are ACL problems: a consuming principal needs Read on the topic AND Read on its group. Authentication succeeded (otherwise the error would be an authentication failure).
optionNotes: Partition count doesn't cause an authorization exception.
Correct — TopicAuthorizationException means the principal is missing Read ACLs on the topic (and its group).
A wrong codec causes deserialization issues, not an authorization error.
auto.offset.reset affects starting position, not authorization.

questions.q: What replaaced ZooKeeper's role in modern Kafka clusters (Kafka 4.x)?
options: An external etcd cluster manages the metadata
KRaft — an internal Raft-based controller quorum managing cluster metadata
A relational database is what now stores all of the cluster metadata in modern Kafka
The Schema Registry took over metadata duties
questions.explanation: KRaft mode has Kafka manage its own metadata via a Raft consensus quorum of controllers — no external ZooKeeper. Client APIs are unaffected; it's a cluster architecture change.
optionNotes: Kafka does not use etcd for metadata.
Correct — KRaft replaces ZooKeeper with an internal Raft controller quorum for metadata.
Metadata is not stored in an external RDBMS.
Schema Registry manages schemas, not cluster metadata.

questions.q: Partitions are added to an existing topic whose producers use keyed messages. What is the consequence?
options: Nothing changes — Kafka automatically rebalances all of the existing data across the new partitions
New records for a key may hash to a different partition, breaking per-key ordering at the boundary
All previously stored data in the topic is deleted
Producers must be restarted before any records will flow
questions.explanation: Partitioning is hash(key) % numPartitions — changing numPartitions changes the mapping. Existing data stays where it was, so a key's history spans partitions and ordered consumption per key is broken across the change. Plan partition counts up front for keyed topics.
optionNotes: Kafka does not move existing records when partitions are added.
Correct — adding partitions changes the hash mapping, so a key's new records may land elsewhere, breaking ordering.
Existing data is retained, not deleted.
Producers pick up the new partitions via metadata refresh, no restart required.

questions.q: What are Kafka message headers for?
options: They determine which partition a given record is ultimately written to by the producer's partitioner
They carry metadata (correlation IDs, trace context, schema hints) alongside key and value
They store the record's offset within the partition
They hold the compressed form of the payload
questions.explanation: Headers are key/value metadata attached to records — used for tracing, routing hints, and provenance. Partitioning uses the message key, not headers.
optionNotes: Partitioning is driven by the key/partitioner, not headers.
Correct — headers carry side-band metadata (tracing, routing hints) without affecting key, value, or partitioning.
Offsets are assigned by the broker, not stored in headers.
Compression applies to the batch, not to a header field.

questions.q: What does enable.idempotence=true on a producer actually prevent?
options: Duplicates caused by the application code itself calling send() twice for one record
Duplicates from the producer's own retries after transient failures, keeping ordering
Duplicates on the consumer side after a group rebalance
All duplicates anywhere across the end-to-end pipeline
questions.explanation: Idempotence gives each producer session sequence numbers so broker-side retry duplicates are discarded. It does NOT dedupe application-level re-sends nor consumer-side reprocessing — those need transactions or idempotent consumers.
optionNotes: App-level double-sends are the application's bug; idempotence can't see them.
Correct — idempotent producer dedupes retries of the same batch and preserves per-partition order.
Consumer-side duplicates are a commit/EOS concern, not producer idempotence.
It only covers producer retries, not the whole pipeline.

questions.q: A consume-transform-produce application must achieve exactly-once semantics from input topic to output topic. What is required?
options: Setting enable.idempotence=true on the producer, which is sufficient for exactly-once on its own
Kafka transactions: a transactional.id producer writing output and committing offsets atomically (read_committed)
Using acks=all together with retries=0
A manual commitSync() after each processed record
questions.explanation: EOS for read-process-write means the output records and the input offset commit are atomic — that's sendOffsetsToTransaction inside a transaction. read_committed consumers then ignore aborted attempts. Idempotence alone only covers producer retry duplicates.
optionNotes: Idempotence dedupes writes but doesn't tie offset commits to output atomically.
Correct — EOS across topics needs transactions (transactional.id + offsets in the txn) and read_committed downstream.
acks/retries affect durability, not atomic consume-transform-produce.
Manual commits are at-least-once, not exactly-once.

questions.q: A consumer with enable.auto.commit=true crashes AFTER auto-commit ran but BEFORE it finished processing the last polled batch. What happens to those records?
options: They are redelivered to the group after the consumer restarts
They are lost to this group — the committed offset already moved past them
They are automatically moved to a dead-letter queue
The broker detects the gap and reprocesses them
questions.explanation: Auto-commit can commit offsets for records not yet processed, creating an at-most-once window. To get at-least-once, commit manually AFTER processing (commitSync/commitAsync).
optionNotes: The offset was already committed, so they won't be redelivered.
Correct — auto-commit advanced the offset before processing finished, so those records are skipped (at-most-once risk).
Kafka has no built-in DLQ for this; it just moves the offset.
Brokers don't reprocess based on client processing state.

questions.q: A consumer processing takes up to 10 minutes per batch, and the group keeps rebalancing with 'consumer left the group' messages. Which setting is being violated?
options: session.timeout.ms — heartbeats are being missed
max.poll.interval.ms — too long between poll() calls; raise it or lower max.poll.records
auto.offset.reset is set incorrectly
linger.ms is configured far too high on the producer side, delaying every single batch it sends out
questions.explanation: Heartbeats run on a background thread, so session.timeout isn't the issue — the poll-interval timer is. If poll() isn't called within max.poll.interval.ms (default 5 min), the consumer is evicted. Raise the limit, shrink batches, or speed up processing.
optionNotes: Heartbeats run on a background thread, so long processing usually trips max.poll.interval first.
Correct — 10-minute batches exceed max.poll.interval.ms; the coordinator evicts the consumer. Raise it or shrink batches.
auto.offset.reset controls start position, not rebalances.
linger.ms is a producer batching setting, unrelated here.

questions.q: When does auto.offset.reset=earliest take effect?
options: On every single consumer restart
Only when the group has no valid committed offset (new group, expired, or out-of-range)
Whenever the consumer's lag on any partition climbs above a preconfigured threshold value at runtime
Every time the group performs a rebalance
questions.explanation: auto.offset.reset is the FALLBACK position. With valid committed offsets, consumption always resumes from them — earliest/latest only applies when no usable offset exists.
optionNotes: A restarting consumer with committed offsets resumes from them, not from earliest.
Correct — the reset policy applies only when there is no valid committed offset to resume from.
Lag doesn't trigger an offset reset.
Rebalances reassign partitions but keep committed offsets.

questions.q: Which producer configuration change increases throughput at the cost of a little latency?
options: Set acks=0 so the producer never waits
Raise linger.ms and batch.size so larger, better-compressed batches are sent
Decrease buffer.memory to force the producer into using smaller, more frequent batches
Set max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=1
questions.explanation: linger.ms deliberately waits to fill batches; bigger batches amortize request overhead and compress better — the standard throughput knob. acks=0 trades DURABILITY (not just latency) and isn't a batching improvement.
optionNotes: acks=0 boosts throughput but sacrifices durability, not the intended trade-off.
Correct — a little linger plus bigger batches amortizes overhead and improves compression, trading slight latency for throughput.
Shrinking buffer.memory reduces batching and can stall the producer.
max.in.flight=1 lowers throughput to preserve ordering.

questions.q: In Schema Registry, subject 'orders-value' uses BACKWARD compatibility. What does registering a new schema version require, and which side upgrades first?
options: The new schema must be readable by the old schema, so the producers are the side that must upgrade first
Consumers on the NEW schema must read data written with the PREVIOUS schema; consumers upgrade first
Schemas can never change once registered
Both producers and consumers must upgrade simultaneously
questions.explanation: BACKWARD = new reader, old data. Consumers deploy the new schema first and can still read what current producers write; then producers may upgrade. FORWARD is the reverse (producers first); FULL is both.
optionNotes: That reverses BACKWARD's direction and upgrade order.
Correct — BACKWARD means new-schema consumers can read old data, so consumers upgrade first.
Compatibility modes exist precisely to allow safe evolution.
The point of compatibility is to avoid lock-step upgrades.

questions.q: How does a consumer know which Avro schema a record was written with, using the Confluent wire format?
options: The complete Avro schema is embedded inside every single message that is written to the topic on the wire
Each message carries a schema ID; the deserializer fetches and caches that schema from the registry
The topic name encodes which schema was used
The schema is inferred by guessing from the raw bytes
questions.explanation: The wire format is magic byte + 4-byte schema ID + payload — compact messages, with the schema itself stored once in the registry and cached client-side after first fetch.
optionNotes: Embedding the full schema per message would be hugely wasteful — Confluent uses an ID.
Correct — the wire format prefixes a schema ID that the deserializer resolves (and caches) via Schema Registry.
Topic names don't encode schemas.
Deserialization is exact, not guessed.

questions.q: A producer intermittently logs NotLeaderForPartitionException but messages eventually arrive. What is happening?
options: The topic is corrupt and must be deleted and then recreated from scratch to recover from the recurring leadership errors
A retriable error: leadership moved (broker restart/rebalance); the producer refreshes metadata and retries
The producer's serializer is misconfigured or broken
The message exceeded max.request.size and was rejected
questions.explanation: Leadership changes are normal transient cluster events — classic RETRIABLE errors the client handles with retries + metadata refresh. Serialization and record-too-large errors are non-retriable and would fail every attempt.
optionNotes: Nothing is corrupt; leadership simply moved.
Correct — NotLeaderForPartition is transient during leader changes; the client refreshes metadata and retries automatically.
A serializer fault would fail consistently, not intermittently with eventual success.
An oversize record raises RecordTooLargeException, a different error.

questions.q: A consumer hits a record that always throws SerializationException, blocking the partition. What is the standard remedy?
options: Restart the consumer over and over until the poison record somehow gets through and normal processing continues
Handle the poison record: skip its offset (or use an error-tolerant deserializer) and send it to a DLQ
Delete the entire topic to clear the record
Reduce fetch.max.bytes so the record isn't fetched
questions.explanation: Deserialization failures are non-retriable — the same bytes fail forever, so retrying blocks the partition. Skip-and-DLQ keeps the pipeline flowing while preserving the bad record for investigation.
optionNotes: Restarting hits the same poison record and blocks again.
Correct — advance past the bad offset (or tolerate the deserialization error) and route it to a dead-letter topic.
Deleting the topic destroys all data — far too drastic.
fetch.max.bytes controls batch size, not which records are poison.

questions.q: Which tool shows a consumer group's current offset, log-end offset, and lag per partition from the command line?
options: kafka-topics.sh --describe on the topic
kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group <name>
kafka-console-consumer.sh --from-beginning
kafka-configs.sh --describe on the broker
questions.explanation: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe lists each partition's CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET and LAG (plus member assignments) — also the tool for --reset-offsets. kafka-topics.sh describes topic structure, not group positions.
optionNotes: kafka-topics describes partitions/replicas, not group lag.
Correct — kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe shows current offset, log-end offset, and lag per partition.
The console consumer reads data; it doesn't report lag.
kafka-configs manages configuration, not consumer lag.

questions.q: Where is producer-side compression applied, and what maximizes its benefit?
options: Applied per record on the broker after receipt, so its benefit is essentially fixed
Per batch on the producer (compression.type); larger batches compress better
Only on the consumer side after fetching
Only when Schema Registry is enabled
questions.explanation: The producer compresses whole batches before sending; brokers typically store them compressed and consumers decompress. Because compression works on batch payloads, better batching directly improves ratio and throughput.
optionNotes: Compression is a producer batch operation, not per-record on the broker.
Correct — the producer compresses each batch; bigger batches (linger.ms/batch.size) yield better ratios.
Consumers decompress; they don't originate compression.
Compression is independent of Schema Registry.

questions.q: Which API creates topics, alters configurations, and lists consumer-group offsets programmatically?
options: The Producer API
The AdminClient (Admin API)
The Kafka Streams API
The Connect REST API
questions.explanation: AdminClient is the management API for cluster resources: topics, configs, ACLs, consumer groups and their offsets. The Connect REST API manages connectors only.
optionNotes: The Producer API writes records; it can't manage topics or groups.
Correct — AdminClient creates topics, alters configs, and inspects consumer-group offsets programmatically.
Streams builds processing topologies, not cluster administration.
The Connect REST API manages connectors, not general cluster admin.

questions.q: A Kafka Streams application reads a topic with 8 partitions. The team deploys 10 instances (1 stream thread each) for throughput. What happens?
options: All 10 instances receive tasks and process data in parallel
Only 8 instances get tasks — parallelism is capped by input partitions; 2 stay idle
The application fails to start when there are more instances than input partitions
Kafka automatically adds partitions to use all instances
questions.explanation: Streams creates one task per input partition; tasks are the unit of parallelism distributed across instances/threads sharing the application.id. Beyond 8 active workers there is nothing to assign.
optionNotes: Tasks are bounded by input partitions, so not all 10 can get work.
Correct — one task per input partition caps parallelism at 8; the extra 2 instances stay idle standbys.
Extra instances are fine; they just hold no active tasks.
Streams never auto-adds partitions.

questions.q: Which abstraction models 'the current price of each product, updated over time'?
options: A KStream, where every record is an independent event
A KTable — a changelog where each key holds its latest value
A windowed KStream aggregation
A repartition topic between operators
questions.explanation: Latest-value-per-key semantics is exactly a KTable: new records for a key UPDATE the table. A KStream would treat every price change as a separate immutable event.
optionNotes: A KStream models an unbounded event flow, not current state per key.
Correct — 'current value per key over time' is a KTable (changelog semantics).
A window bounds time; it doesn't model latest-value-per-key.
A repartition topic is plumbing, not a state abstraction.

questions.q: A stream must be enriched with a small reference dataset (200 rows) on a key different from the stream's key, without repartitioning. Which construct fits?
options: A plain KTable join, which requires both inputs to be co-partitioned on exactly the same join key
A GlobalKTable join — fully replicated to each instance, joinable on any key via a key mapper
A windowed stream-stream join
A simple merge of the two KStreams
questions.explanation: GlobalKTables are replicated in full to every instance, so joins don't require co-partitioning and can use an arbitrary join key. Perfect for small lookup data; regular KTable joins need same key + same partition count.
optionNotes: A KTable join needs co-partitioning on matching keys, which we want to avoid.
Correct — a small GlobalKTable is replicated everywhere and joins on any field without repartitioning.
Stream-stream joins are for two event streams in a window, not reference enrichment.
Merge interleaves streams; it doesn't enrich by lookup.

questions.q: What backs a Kafka Streams state store for fault tolerance?
options: A relational database that the user separately configures and points each state store at for durability
A compacted changelog topic in Kafka; state is restored by replaying it (standbys speed recovery)
A periodic snapshot file uploaded to S3
Nothing — state is simply lost on failure
questions.explanation: Every state store writes its updates to a changelog topic. On task migration/failure the store is rebuilt from that topic; num.standby.replicas maintains warm copies to cut restore time.
optionNotes: State stores are backed by Kafka, not an external RDBMS.
Correct — a compacted changelog topic durably backs the store; recovery replays it (standby replicas shorten it).
There is no S3 snapshotting in core Streams fault tolerance.
State is recoverable, not lost.

questions.q: Count user clicks in fixed, non-overlapping 5-minute buckets. Which window type is this?
options: A hopping window of size 5 min advancing every 1 min
A tumbling window of 5 minutes
A session window with a 5-minute inactivity gap
A sliding window over the records
questions.explanation: Tumbling = fixed size, no overlap (each event in exactly one window). Hopping overlaps (size + smaller advance); session windows are activity-based with an inactivity gap; sliding relates records within a time difference.
optionNotes: Hopping windows overlap; these buckets are non-overlapping.
Correct — fixed, non-overlapping, equal-size buckets are tumbling windows.
Session windows are activity-driven, not fixed intervals.
Sliding windows are for join/aggregation over relative time, not fixed buckets.

questions.q: Group user activity into sessions that end after 30 minutes of inactivity. Which window applies?
options: A tumbling 30-minute window
Session windows with a 30-minute inactivity gap
A hopping window advancing every 30 minutes
No windowing is needed for this
questions.explanation: Session windows are dynamic: they extend while events keep arriving and close when the gap (30 min) elapses without activity — precisely the sessionization use case.
optionNotes: Tumbling windows are fixed-time, not gap-of-inactivity based.
Correct — activity separated by a 30-minute idle gap is exactly a session window.
Hopping windows are fixed/overlapping, not inactivity-based.
Sessionization inherently requires windowing.

questions.q: What configuration turns on exactly-once processing in a Kafka Streams application?
options: enable.idempotence=true on the underlying producer
processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2
isolation.level=read_committed on the consumer
acks=all on the producer
questions.explanation: Streams wraps its consume-process-produce cycle (including changelog writes) in transactions when processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 is set — the one-line EOS switch. The other settings are pieces Streams manages internally.
optionNotes: Idempotence is necessary but not the Streams-level EOS switch.
Correct — processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 turns on end-to-end EOS in Streams.
read_committed is a consumer read setting, not the Streams guarantee toggle.
acks=all is durability, not exactly-once processing.

questions.q: Before a stream-stream join, one stream is re-keyed with selectKey(). What does Streams do to make the join correct?
options: It fails at startup, because re-keying a stream immediately before a join is simply not permitted
It writes the re-keyed stream through an internal repartition topic so equal keys share a task
It broadcasts every record to every task
It ignores the new key and joins on the old one
questions.explanation: After a key change, records with the same new key may sit in different partitions. Streams inserts a repartition topic to shuffle them so join/aggregation tasks see all records for a key. (Joins also require equal partition counts — co-partitioning.)
optionNotes: Re-keying is fully supported.
Correct — a repartition topic reshuffles by the new key so matching keys co-locate before the join.
Broadcasting isn't how a keyed join co-locates data.
The new key is honored, not ignored.

questions.q: By default, which notion of time drives Kafka Streams windowing?
options: Wall-clock processing time at the moment the record is handled by the stream thread
Event time from the record's timestamp (customizable via a TimestampExtractor)
The broker's disk-flush time
The time the consumer polled the record
questions.explanation: Streams windows on record timestamps (event time), so out-of-order and late records still fall into their correct windows (within the grace period). Processing time would make results depend on when the app happens to run.
optionNotes: Processing (wall-clock) time is not the default; event time is.
Correct — windowing uses record event time by default, overridable with a TimestampExtractor.
Disk-flush time is unrelated to windowing.
Poll time isn't the windowing clock.

questions.q: A windowed aggregation emits multiple intermediate updates per window, but the sink should receive only one final result per window. What is the idiomatic solution?
options: Poll the input far less frequently so that the aggregation emits fewer intermediate window updates
Use suppress() to hold updates until the window closes, emitting only the final value
Set linger.ms very high on the sink producer
Route the aggregation through a GlobalKTable
questions.explanation: Streams refines window results continuously; suppress(untilWindowCloses) buffers them and forwards only the final result when the window plus grace period ends.
optionNotes: Poll frequency doesn't control intermediate window emissions.
Correct — suppress(untilWindowCloses) buffers and emits only the final per-window result.
linger.ms batches network sends; it doesn't collapse window updates.
A GlobalKTable is for replicated lookups, not final-result suppression.

questions.q: How does a Kafka Streams application scale out at runtime?
options: It cannot scale out without a full redeployment
Start more instances with the SAME application.id — tasks and state migrate automatically
Give each instance a different group.id
Increase the internal changelog topic's replication factor so that more instances can process in parallel
questions.explanation: application.id doubles as the consumer group id: new instances join the group, the tasks rebalance across members, and state stores are rebuilt/migrated (changelogs, standbys). Same mechanism in reverse handles instance loss.
optionNotes: Streams scales elastically at runtime by adding members.
Correct — new instances sharing application.id join the group and receive rebalanced tasks (with their state).
Distinct group.ids would break the shared processing group.
Replication factor is durability, not scaling.

questions.q: What is a Kafka Streams application, operationally?
options: A dedicated server process that must be installed and kept running on each and every Kafka broker in the cluster
A standard Java app using a client library — deployed anywhere, with no processing cluster to install
A plugin that runs inside ZooKeeper
A managed service available only on Confluent Cloud
questions.explanation: Streams is a library, not a cluster: the topology runs inside your own JVM processes, coordinated through Kafka itself. Deployment is ordinary application deployment — which is exactly why it fits Docker/Kubernetes pipelines.
optionNotes: Streams runs in your app, not on the brokers.
Correct — it's a library-based JVM app you deploy like any other (VMs/Docker/K8s); no separate cluster.
It has nothing to do with ZooKeeper internals.
Streams is open-source, not a cloud-only service.

questions.q: Data must be copied continuously from a PostgreSQL database into Kafka, and from Kafka into Elasticsearch, with minimal custom code. What is the recommended approach?
options: Write a fully custom producer and consumer application for each connected system in the integration pipeline
Kafka Connect: a source connector for PostgreSQL and a sink connector for Elasticsearch, config-driven
A Kafka Streams app making JDBC calls directly
A cron job driving the console producer
questions.explanation: This is Kafka Connect's exact purpose: existing connectors handle offsets, retries, scaling, and schemas via configuration. Custom clients re-implement all of that; Streams is for processing, not system integration.
optionNotes: Custom apps are exactly the code Connect exists to avoid.
Correct — Connect's source and sink connectors move data in/out of Kafka with configuration, not custom code.
Streams is for processing, not connector-style ingestion/egress.
A cron+console-producer pipeline is brittle and unscalable.

questions.q: Where does a DISTRIBUTED Kafka Connect cluster store connector configurations, source offsets, and status?
options: In local files stored on each individual worker node across the Connect cluster
In internal Kafka topics (config, offset, status) shared by the worker group
In ZooKeeper alongside cluster metadata
In the Schema Registry
questions.explanation: Distributed mode keeps its state in Kafka itself, which is what makes workers stateless and fault-tolerant — any worker can pick up work. Standalone mode is the one using local files.
optionNotes: Local files are standalone-mode behavior, not distributed.
Correct — distributed Connect persists config/offsets/status in internal Kafka topics shared by the group.
Connect doesn't use ZooKeeper for this.
Schema Registry stores schemas, not connector state.

questions.q: How is a connector created and managed in a distributed Connect cluster?
options: By editing each broker's server.properties file and then restarting all of the brokers in the cluster
Via the Connect REST API — POST the config; also for status, pause/resume, restart, delete
Only through the ZooKeeper command-line tools
By placing a config file on each worker manually
questions.explanation: Distributed Connect is managed at runtime over its REST interface (e.g., POST /connectors, GET /connectors/<name>/status). Standalone workers take properties files at startup instead.
optionNotes: Connectors aren't broker configs.
Correct — the Connect REST API manages the full connector lifecycle.
ZooKeeper CLIs don't manage connectors.
Manual per-worker files are standalone-style, not distributed management.

questions.q: What is the relationship between a connector and its tasks?
options: A connector and a task are really just two interchangeable names for exactly the same underlying thing
The connector is the job definition; it splits work into tasks (up to tasks.max) run across workers
Tasks manage and schedule the workers
One task always maps to exactly one topic
questions.explanation: Connector = coordination + config; tasks = the parallel data-copying units. Effective parallelism is also limited by the source's divisible units (tables, files) or the topic's partitions for sinks.
optionNotes: They are distinct: definition vs. unit of execution.
Correct — the connector defines the job and divides it into tasks (the parallelism unit) spread over workers.
Workers host tasks, not the other way around.
Task-to-topic mapping isn't fixed at one-to-one.

questions.q: A JDBC source connector must write Avro records with schemas managed centrally. What configuration achieves this?
options: The JDBC source connector always emits Avro records by default without any converter config
Set value.converter=AvroConverter with a schema.registry.url — converters are separate from connectors
Use the StringConverter for values
Enable errors.tolerance=all on the connector
questions.explanation: Converters translate Connect's internal records to bytes and are configured separately from the connector logic — the same connector can emit Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema, or plain JSON depending on converter settings.
optionNotes: Format is chosen by the converter, not baked into the connector.
Correct — the Avro converter + registry URL controls serialization independently of the connector.
StringConverter produces plain strings, not schema-managed Avro.
Error tolerance is about bad records, not serialization format.

questions.q: Field 'ssn' must be masked and records routed to a per-region topic as they flow through a connector — no external processing step. What mechanism does Connect provide?
options: A separate, custom Kafka Streams job inserted into the pipeline just before the sink connector runs
Single Message Transforms (SMTs) chained in the connector config (e.g. MaskField + a router)
Broker-side message interceptors
Schema Registry compatibility rules
questions.explanation: SMTs apply lightweight per-record transformations inside the connector pipeline. For heavier logic (joins, aggregation) you'd use a stream processor between source and sink instead.
optionNotes: That adds an external processing step, which the question rules out.
Correct — SMTs mask/route records inline within the connector, no extra app needed.
Brokers don't transform record contents.
Compatibility rules govern schema evolution, not masking/routing.

questions.q: A sink connector keeps dying on malformed records. It must skip them but keep them for later inspection. Which configuration applies?
options: Set errors.tolerance=none so that the very first malformed record immediately stops the whole task
errors.tolerance=all with errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name — failed records go to a DLQ
Set tasks.max=1 to serialize processing
Switch key.converter to StringConverter
questions.explanation: Tolerance 'all' keeps the task alive past bad records, and the DLQ topic (a SINK-connector feature) captures each failure — with reasons in headers when enabled — instead of silently dropping it.
optionNotes: tolerance=none is the failing default behavior, not the fix.
Correct — tolerate errors and route bad records to a DLQ topic (with context headers) for later inspection.
Reducing tasks doesn't handle malformed records.
Changing the converter doesn't quarantine bad records.

questions.q: Compared with a polling JDBC source, what advantage does log-based CDC (e.g., Debezium) provide?
options: It needs no connector or infrastructure at all
It reads the DB transaction log: captures every change (incl. DELETEs) with low latency and little table load
It keeps working even while the source database is completely stopped, since it reads from a cached snapshot instead
It automatically converts all data to Avro
questions.explanation: Polling misses deletes, adds query load, and is bounded by the poll interval. Reading the transaction log streams every committed row change (with before/after images), which is why CDC is preferred for database replication into Kafka.
optionNotes: CDC still runs as a connector (e.g. Debezium).
Correct — log-based CDC captures all changes including deletes, with low latency and minimal query load.
It reads the live DB's log; the DB must be running.
Serialization format is a converter choice, not a CDC feature.

questions.q: One worker in a 3-worker distributed Connect cluster crashes. What happens to the tasks it was running?
options: Its tasks are lost until that worker comes back online
The remaining workers rebalance and resume its tasks from offsets stored in Kafka
All connectors restart from the very beginning of history
The cluster stops accepting REST API calls
questions.explanation: Because config/offsets/status live in Kafka topics, workers are interchangeable: the group rebalances the failed worker's tasks and they resume from their last committed source offsets.
optionNotes: Distributed Connect is fault-tolerant; tasks don't just wait.
Correct — surviving workers take over the failed worker's tasks, resuming from Kafka-stored offsets.
Work resumes from committed offsets, not from scratch.
The remaining workers keep serving the REST API.

questions.q: Which statement about standalone Connect workers is TRUE?
options: They are the recommended choice for production because of their operational simplicity and very low overhead
They run in one process with offsets in a local file — no fault tolerance or scaling; fine for dev/test
They store their offsets in Kafka automatically
They are unable to run source connectors
questions.explanation: Standalone = one process, local offset storage, no failover — a dev/test convenience. Production runs distributed mode for fault tolerance, scaling, and REST management.
optionNotes: Standalone lacks fault tolerance, so it isn't a production recommendation.
Correct — single process, local-file offsets, no HA or scale-out — suitable for development and testing.
Local-file offsets are exactly what makes it non-distributed.
Standalone can run source connectors.

questions.q: Producer callback logic (success metrics, error handling) must be unit-tested without a broker. Which tool fits?
options: A Testcontainers-based real Kafka broker started up specifically for the duration of every test run
MockProducer — records sent messages for assertions and can simulate failures via errorNext
The kafka-console-producer CLI tool
The TopologyTestDriver for Streams
questions.explanation: MockProducer is the in-memory test double from kafka-clients: assert on history(), drive callbacks with simulated success/failure — no broker, no network, deterministic.
optionNotes: Testcontainers spins up a real broker — heavier than a unit test needs.
Correct — MockProducer runs in-process, captures sends, and can inject failures for callback tests.
The console producer is a CLI, not a unit-test harness.
TopologyTestDriver tests Streams topologies, not a plain producer callback.

questions.q: Which combination tests a Kafka Streams topology synchronously without any running Kafka cluster?
options: MockConsumer combined with MockProducer
TopologyTestDriver with TestInputTopic and TestOutputTopic
The kafka-consumer-groups.sh CLI
A three-broker Testcontainers cluster
questions.explanation: TopologyTestDriver executes the topology in-process: pipe records via TestInputTopic, assert results from TestOutputTopic, and query state stores directly — fast, deterministic unit tests for Streams logic.
optionNotes: Mocks test plain clients, not a full topology synchronously.
Correct — TopologyTestDriver with Test I/O topics drives a topology in-process with no broker.
That CLI inspects groups; it doesn't run topologies.
A real cluster is an integration test, not the synchronous in-process option.

questions.q: A tumbling-window aggregation with a grace period must be tested deterministically, including late-arrival behavior. How?
options: Call Thread.sleep() in the test and simply wait until the aggregation window naturally closes on its own
Use TopologyTestDriver's explicit time control — pipe records at chosen timestamps and advance time
Run it in production and observe the outcome
Accept that windows cannot be tested deterministically
questions.explanation: The test driver lets you SET record timestamps and advance time programmatically, so window boundaries, grace periods, and suppress() emissions are exercised deterministically — no sleeps, no flakiness.
optionNotes: Sleeping is flaky and wall-clock dependent.
Correct — the driver lets you set record timestamps and advance time to deterministically close windows.
Testing in production isn't deterministic verification.
Windows are testable precisely because of the driver's time control.

questions.q: Which risks are NOT covered by mocks and the TopologyTestDriver, and therefore need an integration test with a real broker (e.g., Testcontainers)?
options: Branching and split logic within the processing topology itself, which can only run against a live broker
Security handshakes, real Schema Registry (de)serialization, transactions, and rebalance behavior
A simple map() transformation on records
Producer send callback code
questions.explanation: Interaction-level concerns — auth/TLS, registry round-trips, transactional semantics, group coordination — only exist against real infrastructure. Pure logic belongs in fast unit tests; keep both layers.
optionNotes: Branching is pure topology logic the driver covers.
Correct — security, real serialization, transactions, and rebalances need a real broker to exercise.
map() is unit-testable with the driver.
Callbacks are covered by MockProducer.

questions.q: How should schema changes be prevented from breaking production consumers?
options: Deploy the schema change straight to production and simply monitor the downstream consumer logs for any errors afterward
Check candidate schemas against the registry's compatibility mode in CI so incompatible changes fail the build
Disable compatibility checking to avoid friction
Use StringSerializer everywhere to sidestep schemas
questions.explanation: Schema Registry can validate a proposed schema against the subject's BACKWARD/FORWARD/FULL rules before anything is deployed — wiring that check into CI catches breaking changes at build time.
optionNotes: Discovering breakage in production is too late.
Correct — a CI compatibility check (REST endpoint or Maven goal) fails incompatible schemas before release.
Disabling checks removes the very safety net you want.
Dropping schemas abandons the guarantees Schema Registry provides.

questions.q: What design choice makes producer/consumer application code easy to unit test?
options: Instantiate a brand-new KafkaProducer inside every single method that needs to send a record downstream to Kafka
Inject the Producer/Consumer interface into the logic so tests pass mocks and prod passes real clients
Make all the methods static for simplicity
Test the application only through the CLI tools
questions.explanation: Dependency injection against the client INTERFACES is what lets the same logic run with mocks in tests. Hard-coded client construction forces integration tests for everything.
optionNotes: Constructing clients inline makes code hard to isolate in tests.
Correct — dependency injection of the client interface lets tests substitute MockProducer/MockConsumer.
Static methods hinder mocking and injection.
CLI-only testing can't unit-test business logic.

questions.q: What exactly is consumer lag for a partition?
options: The total elapsed wall-clock time since the consumer last called poll() against the broker
Log-end offset minus the consumer's committed offset — how many records behind it is
The number of group rebalances per hour
The number of bytes sitting in the fetch buffer
questions.explanation: Lag counts unconsumed records between the partition's newest offset and the consumer's position. Persistent lag growth is THE signal that consumption can't keep up with production.
optionNotes: Lag is a record count, not a time-since-poll.
Correct — lag = log-end offset − current/committed offset for the partition.
Rebalance frequency is a different health signal.
Fetch buffer bytes are unrelated to lag.

questions.q: How do Kafka clients expose their metrics for collection?
options: They periodically write metrics to CSV files
Via JMX MBeans per client/topic/node, typically scraped by agents like the Prometheus JMX exporter
Only through Confluent Control Center
Kafka client metrics must be instrumented and exported manually from within the application code by the developer
questions.explanation: Producer, consumer, Streams, and Connect all register JMX metrics out of the box; monitoring stacks scrape them. Streams adds thread/task/store metrics with configurable recording levels.
optionNotes: Clients don't emit CSV metrics.
Correct — Kafka clients expose metrics as JMX MBeans, commonly scraped into Prometheus.
Control Center is one consumer of metrics, not the only path.
The client library already exposes metrics; no manual coding needed.

questions.q: Which producer metric indicates messages that ultimately FAILED delivery after exhausting retries, and what should its value be?
options: record-retry-rate; high values are perfectly normal
record-error-rate; it should be about 0 and alerted on otherwise
batch-size-avg; it should be 0
compression-rate-avg; it should be 1
questions.explanation: record-error-rate counts definitive send failures — data loss from the app's perspective, so alert on any sustained non-zero value. record-retry-rate is the early-warning cousin: transient trouble being retried.
optionNotes: Retries can succeed; retry-rate isn't final failure.
Correct — record-error-rate counts sends that failed after retries; it should sit near 0 and be alerted.
batch-size-avg is a tuning metric, not a failure indicator.
compression-rate-avg measures ratio, not delivery failure.

questions.q: Lag grows steadily on exactly ONE of a topic's 12 partitions. What is the most likely cause to investigate first?
options: The whole cluster is undersized and simply needs more brokers added before any single-partition lag will improve
A hot key skewing traffic to that partition (or one slow consumer) — check key distribution and that assignee
The Schema Registry is down
linger.ms is configured too high
questions.explanation: Uniform problems raise lag everywhere; single-partition lag points to skew (one key getting disproportionate traffic) or the specific consumer assigned to it. Key cardinality/hashing is the classic culprit.
optionNotes: One-partition skew points to data/consumer imbalance, not overall sizing.
Correct — a hot key or one slow/stuck consumer explains lag isolated to a single partition.
A registry outage would affect all partitions, not one.
linger.ms is a producer setting unrelated to single-partition lag.

questions.q: Duplicates appear downstream after consumer crashes, even though processing is correct. The metrics show frequent rebalances. What is the explanation?
options: Kafka randomly duplicates data on its own
At-least-once: processed-but-uncommitted records replay after a rebalance — use idempotency or EOS
The producer's compression codec is silently corrupting the batches it sends downstream
The reported consumer lag has gone negative
questions.explanation: Between processing and offset commit there is always a redelivery window under at-least-once. Frequent rebalances widen it. Fixes: idempotent writes keyed on record identity, or exactly-once processing where applicable.
optionNotes: Kafka doesn't duplicate arbitrarily; the model explains it.
Correct — uncommitted-but-processed records replay after a rebalance; fix with idempotent handling or transactions.
Compression doesn't cause logical duplicates.
Negative lag isn't a real condition here.

questions.q: How can telemetry (counting, tracing) be added to every produced and consumed record WITHOUT modifying business code?
options: Fork and patch the Kafka client library itself to add the telemetry hooks into send and receive paths
Configure ProducerInterceptor / ConsumerInterceptor via interceptor.classes to observe records
Add a custom plugin on the broker
Turn on DEBUG logging for the clients
questions.explanation: Interceptors are the client-side extension point for cross-cutting observability (Confluent's monitoring interceptors use it). They see records in the send/consume path with zero business-code changes.
optionNotes: Forking the library is unnecessary and unmaintainable.
Correct — interceptors registered via interceptor.classes hook send/consume without touching business code.
Broker plugins can't instrument client-side records per app.
DEBUG logging is noisy and not structured telemetry.

questions.q: A trace ID must follow one business transaction from the HTTP request, through the producer, into a consumer three services away. Where does the trace context travel in Kafka?
options: In the message key, overwriting the record's original business key with the propagated trace ID
In message HEADERS (e.g. W3C/OpenTelemetry trace context), propagated producer to consumer
Encoded into the topic name itself
It cannot cross a Kafka boundary at all
questions.explanation: Headers are the metadata channel: tracing libraries inject context on produce and extract it on consume, keeping the trace intact — without abusing the key (which would change partitioning!).
optionNotes: Overloading the key would break partitioning by business key.
Correct — trace context travels in headers, carried from producer to consumer across services.
Topic names can't carry per-record trace context.
Trace context propagates fine via headers.

questions.q: A producer starts throwing TimeoutException and bufferpool-wait-time climbs. What is happening?
options: The consumers on the other side of the topic are lagging far behind and back-pressuring the producer
The producer outpaces the cluster: batches fill buffer.memory until send() blocks and times out
Log compaction deleted the target topic
The consumer group performed a rebalance
questions.explanation: buffer.memory fills when the I/O thread can't drain batches fast enough — from broker slowness, network issues, or plain overproduction. Check request-latency and cluster health; consumer lag is unrelated to producer buffering.
optionNotes: Consumer lag doesn't cause producer buffer waits.
Correct — rising bufferpool-wait-time means the producer can't hand off fast enough; the buffer fills and send() times out.
Compaction doesn't delete topics or block producers.
Consumer rebalances don't stall the producer buffer.

questions.q: Which end-to-end health check catches pipeline delay that per-component metrics can miss?
options: Broker CPU utilization
End-to-end latency: compare the record's event timestamp with when the last consumer processes it
The topic's partition count
The number of connectors currently deployed across the whole cluster and their aggregate throughput
questions.explanation: Each hop can look healthy while queueing between hops accumulates delay. Measuring event-time-to-final-processing spans the entire pipeline — the metric that reflects what the business actually experiences.
optionNotes: Broker CPU is a component metric that misses cross-hop delay.
Correct — event-timestamp-to-processing latency catches pipeline delay that per-component metrics hide.
Partition count is static config, not a health signal.
Connector count says nothing about latency.

questions.q: Which statements about Kafka partitions are TRUE? (Select all that apply)
options: Partition count can be increased after topic creation
Partition count can be decreased after topic creation
Increasing partitions changes which partition new keyed records hash to
A partition's records are totally ordered by offset
questions.explanation: Partitions can be added (never removed), each partition is an ordered log, and because partitioning is hash(key) % numPartitions, adding partitions remaps keys — breaking per-key ordering across the change.

questions.q: A topic has replication.factor=3. Where do producers and consumers connect for a given partition?
options: Any replica of the partition, chosen at random
The partition LEADER handles all produces and, by default, consumes; followers only replicate
Producers write to the leader while consumers by default read only from the follower replicas instead
The controller broker handles all client traffic
questions.explanation: All client traffic for a partition goes through its leader; followers fetch to stay in sync and exist for fault tolerance. (Follower fetching exists as an opt-in rack-aware feature, but the default and exam answer is leader.)
optionNotes: Clients don't pick replicas at random; they target the leader.
Correct — the partition leader serves reads and writes by default; followers just replicate.
By default both produce and consume go to the leader (follower fetching is an opt-in feature).
The controller manages metadata, not client data traffic.

questions.q: What happens when a consumer group has 3 consumers subscribed to two topics with 4 partitions each (8 total)?
options: Only 3 of the 8 partitions end up being consumed
The 8 partitions are distributed across the 3 consumers (e.g. 3/3/2); all are consumed
Each of the 3 consumers is assigned all 8 partitions
The group fails to start because consumer count must evenly divide partition count
questions.explanation: The assignor spreads all subscribed partitions over available members; uneven division is fine. Every partition has exactly one owner within the group.
optionNotes: All partitions are assigned; none are skipped.
Correct — the 8 partitions spread across the 3 consumers (uneven is fine); every partition is consumed.
Each partition has a single owner in the group, not all consumers.
There is no requirement that consumers evenly divide partitions.

questions.q: A compacted topic receives: (k1,v1), (k2,v2), (k1,v3), (k2,null). After full compaction, what remains?
options: All four records are retained after compaction
(k1,v3) only — k2's tombstone eventually removes k2 entirely
(k1,v1) and (k2,v2) — the first value per key wins
(k1,v3) and (k2,null) are retained forever
questions.explanation: Compaction keeps the latest value per key: k1→v3. The null value for k2 is a tombstone marking deletion; after the tombstone retention period it disappears too, leaving only k1's latest value.
optionNotes: Compaction keeps the latest value per key, not every record.
Correct — k1 keeps v3 (latest); the k2 tombstone removes k2 after the delete-retention window.
Compaction keeps the latest value, not the first.
Tombstones are eventually purged; they are not retained forever.

questions.q: Which ACLs does a consumer application using group 'billing-app' need to read topic 'payments'? (Select all that apply)
options: Read on topic 'payments'
Read on group 'billing-app'
Write on topic 'payments'
Alter on the cluster
questions.explanation: Consuming needs READ on the topic AND READ on the consumer group (offset commits are group operations). Write/Alter are producer/admin permissions.

questions.q: Given acks=1, what data-loss window exists?
options: None — acks=1 is already fully durable against any kind of broker failure
If the leader acks then fails before followers replicate, that write is lost on failover
Every single produce is at risk of being lost
Only consumers can lose data with acks=1
questions.explanation: acks=1 waits only for the leader's write. A leader crash before follower replication elects a follower without the record — the acknowledged write vanishes. acks=all + min.insync.replicas closes this window.
optionNotes: acks=1 waits only for the leader, so it isn't fully durable.
Correct — a leader crash after acking but before replication loses that record on failover.
Only the unreplicated tail is at risk, not every write.
The loss window is on the produce/replication side, not the consumer.

questions.q: Which producer settings primarily trade LATENCY for THROUGHPUT? (Select all that apply)
options: linger.ms increased from 0 to 50
batch.size increased
compression.type=zstd
acks changed from all to 0
questions.explanation: Waiting to fill bigger batches (linger.ms, batch.size) and batch compression all add a little latency to gain a lot of throughput. acks=0 trades DURABILITY, not just latency — a different axis.

questions.q: A consumer must never lose a record but can tolerate occasional duplicates. Which commit strategy is correct?
options: enable.auto.commit=true with a short auto-commit interval
Process each record FIRST, then commit offsets manually — at-least-once
Commit offsets before processing — at-most-once
Never commit offsets at all
questions.explanation: Commit-after-processing means a crash re-delivers the uncommitted batch (duplicates possible, no loss) — at-least-once. Auto-commit can commit mid-batch before processing completes, opening a loss window.
optionNotes: Auto-commit can advance offsets before processing completes, risking loss.
Correct — commit only after processing gives at-least-once: no loss, possible duplicates (acceptable here).
Committing before processing is at-most-once and can lose records.
Never committing replays everything on every restart.

questions.q: What is required for a producer to participate in transactions? (Select all that apply)
options: A transactional.id set in the producer config
initTransactions() called before the first transaction
isolation.level=read_committed on the producer
Calls wrapped in beginTransaction()/commitTransaction()
questions.explanation: Transactional producers need a stable transactional.id, an initTransactions() handshake, and explicit begin/commit (or abort). isolation.level is a CONSUMER setting — it controls whether readers see uncommitted records.

questions.q: A producer sends a 5 MB message and gets RecordTooLargeException. Which options genuinely address this? (Select all that apply)
options: Raise max.request.size (producer) and message.max.bytes (broker/topic) if justified
Use the claim-check pattern: store the blob externally, send a reference
Enable compression and hope it fits
Set retries=10
questions.explanation: Size limits exist at producer and broker level — raising both works but has operational costs; claim check is the architectural fix; compression MAY bring the payload under the limit (it's applied before the size check on batches). Retrying a non-retriable error changes nothing.

questions.q: In the Confluent Schema Registry, subject 'orders-value' is set to FORWARD compatibility. What does a valid new schema version guarantee, and who upgrades first?
options: New consumers read old data; consumers upgrade first
Data written with the NEW schema is readable by consumers still on the OLD schema; producers upgrade first
It says nothing about the order of upgrades
Compatibility is guaranteed in both directions, forever, once FORWARD is set on the subject
questions.explanation: FORWARD = old readers handle new data (e.g., new fields are added that old readers ignore). Producers can therefore deploy the new schema first. BACKWARD is the mirror image (consumers first).
optionNotes: That describes BACKWARD, not FORWARD.
Correct — FORWARD means old-schema consumers can read new-schema data, so producers upgrade first.
FORWARD does imply a safe upgrade order (producers first).
FORWARD is one-directional, not both-directional.

questions.q: Why does a consumer use the schema ID embedded in each message rather than the 'latest' schema from the registry?
options: The 'latest' schema in the registry is always the wrong one to decode any record written by an older producer version
Records must be decoded with the WRITER'S schema; a topic legitimately holds records of different versions
Schema IDs are simply faster to type than names
The registry has no concept of a latest schema
questions.explanation: Avro decoding requires the writer's schema; the per-message ID identifies exactly which version wrote that record. Compatibility rules then let the reader map it to its own (reader) schema.
optionNotes: 'latest' isn't wrong, it's just not necessarily the writer's version.
Correct — decoding needs the exact writer schema; mixed versions coexist, so the embedded ID is authoritative.
It's about correctness, not convenience.
The registry does have 'latest'; it's just not the right choice for decoding old records.

questions.q: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe shows one partition with CURRENT-OFFSET 1000, LOG-END-OFFSET 5200. What does this mean?
options: The consumer is 1000 records behind on that partition
The consumer has a lag of 4200 records on that partition
The topic contains exactly 1000 records total
The consumer has read ahead of the end of the log
questions.explanation: Lag = LOG-END-OFFSET − CURRENT-OFFSET = 5200 − 1000 = 4200 unconsumed records. Watching whether this number grows or shrinks over time is the core consumption health check.
optionNotes: 1000 is the current offset, not the lag.
Correct — lag = log-end (5200) − current (1000) = 4200 records.
Offsets are positions, not a record count of the topic.
A consumer cannot be ahead of the log-end offset.

questions.q: A Streams app computes 'orders per customer per 1-hour tumbling window'. Which internal Kafka topics does it create? (Select all that apply)
options: A changelog topic backing the windowed aggregation state store
A repartition topic if the stream is re-keyed to customer ID before aggregating
A DLQ topic automatically
Nothing — Streams keeps state only in memory
questions.explanation: Stateful operations are backed by compacted changelog topics (fault tolerance), and re-keying before an aggregation inserts a repartition topic so each task sees all records for its keys. DLQs are not automatic in Streams.

questions.q: What is the difference between a KStream-KTable join and a KStream-KStream join?
options: There is no meaningful difference between them
Table joins enrich each event with the table's current value; stream-stream joins pair events in a window
Stream-table joins require a time window to work
Stream-stream joins never require the two input streams to be co-partitioned on the join key
questions.explanation: A table lookup is point-in-time — no window needed. Two event streams have no 'current value', so their join must bound how far apart in time matching events may occur (windowed). Both require co-partitioning.
optionNotes: They differ fundamentally in windowing and semantics.
Correct — table joins are windowless current-value lookups; stream-stream joins match within a window.
Stream-table joins are windowless.
Stream-stream joins require co-partitioned inputs.

questions.q: A Streams instance crashes. Its tasks had 50 GB of RocksDB state. What minimizes downtime for those tasks on other instances?
options: Nothing can really help here; the entire 50 GB of RocksDB state is simply lost for good the moment the instance dies
num.standby.replicas >= 1 — warm state copies let tasks migrate near-instantly instead of replaying 50 GB
A larger producer batch.size on the app
Enabling exactly_once_v2 processing
questions.explanation: State is recoverable from changelogs, but replaying 50 GB takes time. Standby replicas continuously mirror state on other instances so failover just promotes the standby.
optionNotes: State is recoverable from the changelog; the goal is to avoid a slow replay.
Correct — standby replicas keep warm copies elsewhere, so failover doesn't replay the whole changelog.
batch.size affects producing, not state recovery.
EOS is about correctness, not recovery speed.

questions.q: In Kafka Streams, what happens when a record arrives 5 minutes late (event time) for a 1-hour window with a 10-minute grace period?
options: It is dropped as a late record
It updates its correct, already-formed window because it is within the grace period
It creates a brand-new window of its own
Streams crashes on the late record
questions.explanation: Event-time processing places records into the window their timestamp belongs to; within the grace period, late records still update that window's result. Beyond grace, they are dropped.
optionNotes: Within the grace period it is not dropped.
Correct — a record 5 min late with a 10-min grace still lands in and updates its original window.
It joins the existing window, not a new one.
Late records are handled gracefully, not fatal.

questions.q: Which Connect worker settings/behaviors are DISTRIBUTED-mode only? (Select all that apply)
options: REST API management of connectors
Config/offset/status stored in internal Kafka topics
Automatic task rebalancing when a worker dies
Running a connector at all
questions.explanation: Distributed mode adds REST management, Kafka-backed shared state, and rebalancing/fault tolerance. Standalone can still RUN connectors — just without any of those properties.

questions.q: A JDBC sink connector reads Avro records (Schema Registry) and writes to Postgres. Which config is essential?
options: Set value.converter=StringConverter so that the sink simply reads all of the records as plain strings
value.converter=AvroConverter with value.converter.schema.registry.url pointing at the registry
Set errors.tolerance=none on the sink
Always pin tasks.max=1 for sinks
questions.explanation: The converter must match how the data was written: AvroConverter + registry URL turns the wire format (magic byte + schema ID) back into structured records with schemas — which the JDBC sink needs to build SQL.
optionNotes: StringConverter can't decode Avro records.
Correct — the Avro converter plus the registry URL is what lets the sink read Schema-Registry Avro.
Error tolerance is unrelated to reading Avro.
tasks.max is a scaling knob, not an Avro requirement.

questions.q: Kafka Connect source offsets (e.g., 'which rows have been read') are stored where in distributed mode?
options: In __consumer_offsets, like ordinary consumer groups
In the Connect cluster's offset storage topic (connect-offsets)
In a local file on each worker
In the Schema Registry
questions.explanation: Source progress is Connect-specific state kept in the connect-offsets internal topic (distributed mode). __consumer_offsets stores consumer-group offsets — sink connectors use that mechanism for their consumer side.
optionNotes: Source offsets are Connect-specific, not consumer-group offsets.
Correct — distributed source offsets live in the connect-offsets internal topic.
Local files are standalone-mode behavior.
Schema Registry stores schemas, not source offsets.

questions.q: During peak load, a sink connector falls behind. tasks.max=1 and the topic has 8 partitions. What is the FIRST scaling step?
options: Add many more partitions to the source topic first, before attempting any other scaling step at all
Raise tasks.max (up to 8, the partition count) so tasks consume in parallel across workers
Add more brokers to the cluster
Reduce the topic's replication factor
questions.explanation: One task = one consumer doing all the work. Raising tasks.max lets Connect run parallel tasks (bounded by partitions for sinks). Partitions/brokers/RF are cluster-side changes that don't fix a self-imposed parallelism cap.
optionNotes: Adding partitions is disruptive and not the first, cheapest step.
Correct — with 8 partitions, raising tasks.max up to 8 parallelizes the sink across workers immediately.
More brokers doesn't help a single-task sink keep up.
Lowering replication reduces durability and doesn't add throughput.

questions.q: Which assertions can a TopologyTestDriver-based test make? (Select all that apply)
options: Values read from a TestOutputTopic match expectations
Contents of a state store at a given point
Window results after advancing the driver's wall-clock time
Actual network latency to the broker
questions.explanation: The driver runs the topology in-process: outputs, state stores, and time-driven behavior (windows, punctuators) are all synchronously inspectable. There IS no broker or network — that's the point.

questions.q: A test must verify the application's error handling when a produce fails with a broker exception. Which approach is deterministic?
options: Run the whole application against production and simply wait for a real broker failure to occur naturally
MockProducer with errorNext(new TimeoutException(...)) to force the send callback to fail
Physically disconnect the network cable mid-test
Set acks=0 and hope a failure occurs
questions.explanation: MockProducer lets the test inject an exact failure into the next send, driving the error path deterministically — no broker, no flakiness, repeatable in CI.
optionNotes: Waiting for real failures is non-deterministic.
Correct — MockProducer.errorNext injects a specific exception so the failure path runs deterministically.
Yanking the network isn't a repeatable unit test.
acks=0 changes durability, not deterministic failure injection.

questions.q: Where should schema-compatibility verification run to prevent breaking consumers?
options: Only manually, just before major releases
In CI on every schema change — validating the candidate against the subject's compatibility mode
Inside the consumer at runtime
Nowhere — the registry already blocks incompatible schemas at produce time, so CI checks add nothing
questions.explanation: The registry does reject incompatible registrations at runtime — but discovering that during deployment is late. CI checks catch it at build time. Runtime consumer failures are exactly what this prevents.
optionNotes: Manual pre-release checks miss most changes.
Correct — automated CI compatibility checks on every change catch breaks before they ship.
Runtime consumer checks are too late to prevent deployment.
Relying on produce-time rejection lets bad changes reach production first.

questions.q: Which metrics indicate the PRODUCER is struggling, before data loss occurs? (Select all that apply)
options: Rising record-retry-rate
Rising request-latency-avg
bufferpool-wait-time climbing (buffer.memory pressure)
records-lag-max
questions.explanation: Retries, broker latency, and buffer pressure are the producer's early-warning chain (ending in TimeoutExceptions). records-lag-max is a CONSUMER metric — the other side of the pipeline.

questions.q: Consumer lag on all partitions grows during business hours and shrinks overnight. Processing each record takes 80 ms, and the topic gets 100 records/second across 4 partitions with 4 consumers. What is the bottleneck?
options: The brokers are the bottleneck
80 ms/record caps 4 consumers near 50 rec/s vs 100/s in — process faster or add parallelism
The network path between the clients and the brokers is the true throughput bottleneck here
The Schema Registry is the constraint
questions.explanation: Capacity math: each consumer handles ~12.5 records/s at 80 ms/record; four handle ~50/s against 100/s inflow — lag grows exactly when traffic exceeds capacity. Fix processing speed or raise parallelism (needs more partitions).
optionNotes: Brokers aren't implicated by this arithmetic.
Correct — 80 ms/record caps 4 consumers near 50 rec/s against 100 rec/s in; speed up or parallelize processing.
The network isn't the limiter here.
Schema Registry isn't the throughput bottleneck.

questions.q: A team wants per-record processing counts and trace propagation WITHOUT modifying application business code. Which Kafka client feature does this?
options: Broker-side plugins installed on every broker across the whole cluster
Producer/Consumer interceptors registered via interceptor.classes
A custom partitioner implementation
A higher fetch.max.bytes setting
questions.explanation: Interceptors hook the send/consume path for cross-cutting concerns (metrics, tracing headers). Partitioners route records; fetch sizes are throughput tuning; brokers can't see application semantics.
optionNotes: Broker plugins don't instrument per-app client records.
Correct — interceptors add counting/tracing at send and consume without changing business code.
A partitioner routes records; it doesn't add telemetry.
fetch.max.bytes is a fetch-size tuning, not instrumentation.

questions.q: End-to-end latency (event created → final consumer processed) is 45 s although every service's own metrics look healthy. Where does the time usually hide, and how is it found?
options: In DNS resolution between services
As QUEUEING between hops (lag at intermediate topics) — found by comparing event vs processing time per stage
In TLS handshakes on each connection
In log-segment rotation on the brokers, which quietly stalls the pipeline whenever segments roll over under load
questions.explanation: Each hop can be fast while records WAIT in topics between hops. Event-timestamp-to-processing-time deltas per stage (or per-group lag) expose which queue is absorbing the 45 seconds.
optionNotes: DNS wouldn't account for 45 s of end-to-end delay.
Correct — the time hides as queueing/lag at intermediate topics; per-stage timestamp comparison locates it.
TLS handshakes are milliseconds, not tens of seconds.
Log rotation doesn't add pipeline latency.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. The cluster has 3 brokers. Broker 2 crashes. Shortly afterwards broker 3 also crashes. What happens to producers writing to this topic with the configuration shown?
questions.exhibit:
Topic: payments
  partitions: 3
  replication.factor: 3
  min.insync.replicas: 2

Producer:
  acks=all
options: Writes succeeded with one broker down, but with two down they fail (NotEnoughReplicas)
Writes continue normally as long as any single broker stays alive, since the leader can always acknowledge on its own
All writes stopped as soon as the first broker crashed
The topic is deleted once fewer brokers than the replication factor remain
optionNotes: Correct. With acks=all, the leader acknowledges only when all in-sync replicas have the record, and min.insync.replicas=2 requires at least 2 ISR members. One broker down leaves 2 in-sync replicas — fine. Two down leaves only the leader (1 < 2), so produce requests fail with NotEnoughReplicas(AfterAppend)Exception.
min.insync.replicas=2 explicitly forbids acknowledging with a lone leader when acks=all.
Losing one of three replicas still satisfies min.insync.replicas=2, so writes kept working.
Topics are never deleted due to broker failures; partitions just become under-replicated or unavailable.
questions.explanation: acks=all + min.insync.replicas=2 + RF=3 is the standard durability recipe: it tolerates one broker failure while guaranteeing every acknowledged record exists on at least two brokers. The trade-off is availability: a second failure makes the partition reject writes rather than risk data loss.

questions.q: A topic has 3 partitions. A consumer group subscribed to it starts a 4th consumer instance. What is the resulting assignment?
options: One consumer stays idle — partitions aren't shared, so at most 3 consumers get an assignment
