Each of the 4 consumers gets 75% of one partition
The 4th consumer triggers automatic creation of a 4th partition to balance the group
All 4 consumers receive all records (broadcast)
optionNotes: Correct. Within a group, a partition is consumed by exactly one member. Three partitions can occupy at most three consumers; the fourth stays idle as a hot standby that takes over on a rebalance.
Partitions are the atomic unit of assignment — they are never split among group members.
Consumers never change topic partition counts; that is an administrative operation.
Broadcast happens across different groups, not within one group.
questions.explanation: Partition count caps a consumer group's parallelism. Extra members beyond the partition count sit idle (useful only as standby), which is why partition count is sized with target consumer parallelism in mind.

questions.q: An application produces click events for user sessions to a 6-partition topic and requires that all events of one session are consumed in the order they were produced. How is this achieved?
options: Use the session ID as the record key so all of a session's events land in the same partition
Set acks=all on the producer
Use a single consumer instance with a very large fetch size to read every session strictly in order
Enable log compaction on the topic
optionNotes: Correct. Kafka guarantees order only within a partition. Keying by session ID makes the default partitioner hash every event of that session to the same partition, preserving per-session order while keeping 6-way parallelism across sessions.
acks controls durability of acknowledgement, not routing or ordering.
One consumer still reads 6 partitions in interleaved order; cross-partition order remains undefined.
Compaction controls retention of latest values per key; it does not create ordering guarantees.
questions.explanation: Ordering in Kafka is a per-partition property. The idiom for entity-level ordering is: key by the entity ID, let the hash partitioner give each entity a stable partition, and order within the entity follows automatically.

questions.q: Where does a Kafka consumer group's committed offset information live by default?
options: In the internal __consumer_offsets topic on the Kafka cluster
In a local file on each consumer host
In ZooKeeper znodes, which all modern Kafka versions still rely on for offsets
In the first record of each partition
optionNotes: Correct. Committed offsets are written to the compacted internal topic __consumer_offsets, keyed by (group, topic, partition) — replicated and durable like any other topic data.
Local files would be lost with the host and could not survive consumer replacement.
ZooKeeper-based offsets are legacy (pre-0.9); modern clients commit to the internal topic, and new clusters run without ZooKeeper entirely (KRaft).
Offsets are consumer-side bookkeeping, not part of partition data.
questions.explanation: __consumer_offsets is a compacted internal topic holding the latest committed offset per group-topic-partition. Compaction keeps exactly the newest commit per key, which is all a group needs to resume.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. After compaction has run on this topic, which records can a new consumer reading from the beginning still receive?
options: userA=Faro and userC=Braga; the older userA record is dropped and the tombstone deletes userB
All five records, because compaction never deletes anything
Only the tombstone, because compaction keeps the newest record overall
userA=Lisbon and userB=Porto, because compaction keeps the oldest record it has seen per key
optionNotes: Correct. Compaction retains at least the latest value per key: userA's Lisbon record is superseded by Faro; userC keeps Braga. A null value is a tombstone — it marks userB deleted and, after delete.retention.ms, the tombstone itself is purged, removing userB from the log.
Compaction exists precisely to delete superseded values per key.
Compaction is per key, not global; each key's newest value survives.
It keeps the newest per key, not the oldest.
questions.explanation: Log compaction turns a topic into a durable latest-value-per-key changelog: superseded values are removed, and null-value tombstones delete keys (remaining visible for delete.retention.ms so consumers can observe the deletion). Ideal for state snapshots like the tables backing Kafka Streams.

questions.q: The leader replica of a partition fails. unclean.leader.election.enable is false (the default). Which replica may become the new leader, and what does this protect against?
options: Only an in-sync-replica-set member, which prevents losing acknowledged records
Any replica, including lagging ones; this maximizes availability
No replica may take over — the partition stays offline until the original leader broker returns
The replica on the broker with the lowest ID, regardless of sync state
optionNotes: Correct. With unclean election disabled, leadership can pass only to an ISR member — a replica guaranteed to have every committed record. This preserves acknowledged data at the cost of availability if the whole ISR is gone.
Electing an out-of-sync replica is exactly the 'unclean' election that the false setting forbids, because it silently discards committed records.
If other ISR members exist, one takes over immediately; the partition goes offline only when no ISR replica survives.
Broker ID plays no role in eligibility; sync state does.
questions.explanation: The ISR is the set of replicas caught up with the leader. Restricting elections to it (unclean.leader.election.enable=false) is the consistency-over-availability stance: committed records survive leader failure, but losing the entire ISR renders the partition unavailable rather than lossy.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What behaviour does this producer configuration provide?
options: Retries can no longer duplicate or reorder within a partition — each record is written once
Exactly-once delivery to consumers, end to end, with no further configuration
The producer stops batching records and sends one at a time
Duplicates are still written on every retry, but consumers are expected to deduplicate them automatically
optionNotes: Correct. Idempotence gives each producer a PID and per-partition sequence numbers; brokers discard duplicates from retries and, with in-flight ≤ 5, preserve ordering. Acknowledged records land exactly once in the partition log.
Idempotence covers the produce path only. End-to-end exactly-once additionally needs transactions on the producer and read_committed consumers (or Streams EOS).
Batching (batch.size/linger.ms) is unaffected by idempotence.
The whole point is that brokers reject the duplicates; consumers never see them.
questions.explanation: The idempotent producer eliminates the classic retry hazards — duplicates and reordering — using producer IDs and sequence numbers checked by the broker. It is produce-side exactly-once; cross-application exactly-once requires the transactional API on top.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. Which delivery semantics does this consumer implement?
options: At-least-once: a crash between process() and commitSync() reprocesses the batch on restart
At-most-once: records can be lost but never reprocessed
Exactly-once, because commitSync() runs atomically together with the record processing
No delivery guarantee, because auto-commit is disabled
optionNotes: Correct. Offsets are committed only after processing succeeds. If the consumer dies mid-batch, the last commit still points before the batch, so those records are delivered again — duplicates are possible, loss is not. process() should therefore be idempotent.
At-most-once is the opposite pattern: commit before processing, risking loss instead of duplication.
commitSync commits offsets; it cannot atomically include an external database write. Exactly-once needs transactions or idempotent sinks.
Disabling auto-commit and committing manually after processing is precisely how a guarantee (at-least-once) is established.
questions.explanation: Commit-after-process = at-least-once; commit-before-process = at-most-once. The gap between an external side effect and the offset commit can never be closed by the consumer API alone — that is what idempotent processing or the transactional/EOS machinery addresses.

questions.q: A consumer processes records in a loop that sometimes takes 8 minutes per batch. With max.poll.interval.ms at its default of 300000 (5 minutes), what happens, and what are the two standard remedies?
options: The coordinator evicts the consumer and rebalances; raise max.poll.interval.ms or lower max.poll.records
The broker throttles the consumer's fetches until it speeds up; the fix is to raise fetch.max.bytes substantially
Nothing — max.poll.interval.ms only applies to consumers using auto-commit
The consumer crashes with an OutOfMemoryError; fix by increasing heap
optionNotes: Correct. Failing to call poll() within max.poll.interval.ms tells the coordinator the consumer is stuck: it is removed from the group and its partitions are reassigned (often causing duplicate processing). Remedies: allow a longer interval, or shrink the per-poll workload (max.poll.records) — or hand off heavy work to another thread while the poll loop keeps polling.
There is no such throttling mechanism tied to poll cadence.
The liveness check applies regardless of the commit mode.
Slow processing does not by itself exhaust memory.
questions.explanation: max.poll.interval.ms is the processing-liveness contract: poll at least this often or be presumed dead. Long-running batches must either fit within it (fewer records per poll) or the interval must be raised — otherwise rebalance storms and duplicate work follow.

questions.q: A producer writes records with StringSerializer for both key and value. A consumer reads the topic configured with IntegerDeserializer for the value. What happens at the consumer?
options: Deserialization fails (SerializationException) — the bytes aren't a 4-byte integer
Kafka transparently converts the string to an integer on the broker before delivery to the consumer
The consumer receives the string's hash code as an integer
The record is silently skipped by the client library
optionNotes: Correct. Brokers store and return opaque bytes; interpretation happens entirely in the client. UTF-8 string bytes are not a valid fixed-width integer encoding, so the deserializer throws, typically breaking the poll loop unless handled (or unless an error-tolerant wrapper is used).
Brokers never inspect or convert payload bytes — serialization is a client-side contract.
Deserializers parse bytes; they do not fall back to hashing.
Plain consumers do not skip undeserializable records by default; the exception surfaces to the application.
questions.explanation: Serialization is an agreement between producers and consumers that Kafka itself never enforces — brokers ship bytes. Mismatched serializers therefore fail at deserialization time, which is exactly the failure mode schema registries and contracts exist to prevent.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What is the difference between the two produce calls?
options: A is asynchronous (send returns a Future, batching in the background); B blocks on get() until ack
A discards any delivery errors silently, while B is the only way to ever detect a failed send
A sends without a key; B includes the key
They are identical because get() is a no-op on completed futures
optionNotes: Correct. send() always hands the record to the background sender and returns a Future immediately; calling get() waits synchronously for the RecordMetadata (or exception). Blocking per record forfeits batching and pipelining — throughput drops dramatically.
A does not discard errors — attach a callback (send(record, callback)) to observe results without blocking.
Keys come from the ProducerRecord, not from how the future is consumed.
get() materially changes behaviour: it blocks until the send completes.
questions.explanation: The producer is asynchronous by design; per-record .get() turns it into a slow synchronous client. The idiomatic way to handle outcomes at full speed is the completion callback, reserving blocking waits for cases that truly need per-record confirmation.

questions.q: Records are produced to a 4-partition topic with a null key. How does the default partitioner distribute them (Kafka 2.4+)?
options: Sticky batching: a batch fills to one random partition, then switches, evening out over time
All null-key records go to partition 0
The broker rejects records without a key
Strictly one record to each partition in round-robin order, regardless of any batching behaviour
optionNotes: Correct. The sticky partitioner sends consecutive null-key records to one partition until its batch is full or sent, then picks another — better batching (fewer, fuller requests) than per-record round-robin, with even long-run distribution.
Null-key traffic is spread across partitions, not pinned to one.
Keys are optional; null keys simply forgo per-key placement.
Per-record round-robin was the old behaviour; it fragmented batches and was replaced.
questions.explanation: With no key there is no placement contract, so the partitioner optimizes for throughput: the sticky strategy batches consecutive records to the same partition and rotates between batches, balancing load without sacrificing batch efficiency.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What guarantee does this combination provide that the idempotent producer alone does not?
options: Atomic consume-transform-produce: output and offset commit succeed or abort together (read_committed)
Faster producing overall, because transactions let the producer skip broker acknowledgements
Encryption of the records inside the transaction
Ordering across different partitions of the output topic
optionNotes: Correct. The transactional API binds the produced records and the consumed offsets into one atomic unit: on failure both are aborted, so reprocessing produces no observable duplicates. read_committed consumers only ever see committed transactional data — this is Kafka's exactly-once processing pattern.
Transactions add coordinator round-trips; they cost a little latency rather than saving it.
Transactions are about atomicity, not confidentiality — encryption is TLS/at-rest configuration.
Kafka never orders across partitions; transactions do not change that.
questions.explanation: Idempotence deduplicates a single producer's retries; transactions extend that to atomic multi-partition writes tied to the consumed offsets. Together with read_committed readers this closes the consume-transform-produce loop into exactly-once semantics — the machinery behind Streams EOS.

questions.q: A producer sends many small records, and profiling shows one network request per record. Which two settings are tuned together to improve throughput via batching, and what is the trade-off?
options: batch.size and linger.ms tuned together; the trade-off is up to linger.ms of extra latency
fetch.min.bytes and session.timeout.ms tuned together; the trade-off is much slower group rebalances
acks and retries; the trade-off is durability
max.poll.records and heartbeat.interval.ms; the trade-off is consumer lag
optionNotes: Correct. A non-zero linger.ms makes the producer wait briefly so batches (bounded by batch.size) fill before sending — fewer, larger requests, better compression, higher throughput, at the cost of that small wait. linger=0 sends as fast as possible with tiny batches.
Those are consumer/group settings, unrelated to producer batching.
acks/retries govern durability semantics, not batch formation.
Also consumer-side settings; the question is about the producer's request pattern.
questions.explanation: Producer batching is the linger.ms/batch.size dial: trade microseconds-to-milliseconds of latency for dramatically fewer requests and better compression. It is the first tuning knob for small-record, high-rate producers.

questions.q: In Kafka Streams, what is the semantic difference between reading a topic as a KStream versus as a KTable?
options: A KStream is a stream of independent events; a KTable upserts, keeping the latest value per key
A KStream is bounded; a KTable is infinite
KTables cannot be joined, only aggregated
A KStream always persists its data on disk, whereas a KTable is held purely in memory at all times
optionNotes: Correct. KStream = record stream: two records with the same key are two facts (e.g. two purchases). KTable = changelog view: a later record with the same key replaces the earlier one (e.g. current address), and null values delete the key.
Both abstractions are unbounded; the difference is interpretation, not size.
KTables join in several ways (table-table, stream-table enrichment).
Both are backed by topics; tables additionally materialize into (disk-backed RocksDB) state stores.
questions.explanation: The stream/table duality: a stream is a sequence of immutable events, a table is the evolving latest-state per key that such a sequence implies. Choosing the right reading of a topic (events vs upserts) drives which joins and aggregations make sense.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. Why does this topology create an internal repartition topic, and what would avoid it?
options: groupBy re-keys to customerId, forcing a repartition before counting; groupByKey avoids it
count() always forces an internal repartition topic to be created regardless of the current key
The topology is invalid; groupBy cannot be followed by count()
Repartition topics appear only when exactly-once is enabled
optionNotes: Correct. Aggregations are computed per key within partitions. Since the new key (customerId) does not match the current partitioning (by orderId), Streams inserts a repartition topic to co-locate each customer's records on one task. If the data were already keyed by customerId, groupByKey() would aggregate with no shuffle.
count() after groupByKey() on an unchanged key needs no repartitioning.
groupBy(...).count() is a perfectly standard aggregation.
EOS changes transactional behaviour, not shuffle topology.
questions.explanation: Any key-changing operation (groupBy, selectKey, map that alters the key) followed by a stateful operation forces a shuffle through an internal repartition topic, doubling the write path. Designing topic keys to match downstream aggregation keys is the main lever for avoiding it.

questions.q: A Kafka Streams application must guarantee that its stateful processing produces no duplicated results in its output topics, even across failures and restarts. Which single configuration enables this?
options: processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2
enable.auto.commit=false
cleanup.policy=compact on each of the output topics to remove duplicate keys
num.stream.threads=1
optionNotes: Correct. exactly_once_v2 makes Streams wrap consuming, state-store updates (changelogs), and producing in Kafka transactions, so each input record's effects are committed atomically exactly once — the supported EOS switch for modern brokers.
Streams manages its own commits; this consumer flag is not how Streams EOS is configured.
Compaction shapes retention of the output; it neither prevents nor removes duplicated aggregate results.
Single-threading reduces parallelism but failures and restarts can still replay uncommitted work.
questions.explanation: Streams exposes exactly-once as one setting: processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2. Under the hood it is the transactional producer + offsets-in-transaction + read_committed pattern, applied automatically to the whole topology including state store changelogs.

questions.q: A Kafka Streams application consumes one input topic with 8 partitions. The team deploys 12 identical instances of the application. How many instances actively process data?
options: 8 — parallelism is bounded by input partitions; the other 4 instances stay idle standbys
12 — Streams simply splits each partition's records among all of the running instances
1 — only the first instance receives tasks
12 — but each processes every record (broadcast)
optionNotes: Correct. Streams creates one task per input partition (8 tasks here) and distributes tasks across instances/threads. With more instances than tasks, the surplus get nothing to do — useful only as warm spares.
There are only 8 units of work to hand out; 12 instances cannot all be busy.
Tasks are balanced across available instances, not concentrated on one.
Within the application (one consumer group), records are partitioned, never broadcast.
questions.explanation: Task count = input partition count is the scaling law of Kafka Streams. Capacity planning starts with partitions: to use 12 instances productively, the input topic needs at least 12 partitions.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does this Kafka Connect configuration do?
options: Polls the customers table and publishes rows with a growing id to crm-customers; updates are missed
Streams every change — inserts, updates, and deletes — from the table using its transaction log
Copies records from the topic crm-customers into the customers table
Takes a one-time snapshot of the table and then stops
optionNotes: Correct. A JDBC source in incrementing mode tracks the highest id it has published and selects only rows above it on each poll. That captures inserts only; updates don't change id and are invisible (timestamp or timestamp+incrementing mode addresses that; true CDC needs a log-based connector such as Debezium).
Log-based change data capture is a different connector family; the JDBC source only polls with queries.
Topic-to-table is a sink connector; this is a source.
The connector keeps polling on its interval, continuously picking up new rows.
questions.explanation: JDBC source modes matter: incrementing = inserts only (monotonic column), timestamp = rows whose timestamp advanced, timestamp+incrementing = both. Query-based capture always trails and misses deletes — the standard reason to step up to log-based CDC.

questions.q: How does Kafka Connect in distributed mode store its configuration, progress, and status — and how is a connector's throughput scaled?
options: In internal Kafka topics; throughput scales by raising tasks.max across the worker cluster
In a shared filesystem visible to every worker; throughput scales only by adding CPU to one worker
In ZooKeeper; throughput scales by adding partitions to the source database
In each worker's local RocksDB; throughput cannot be scaled without downtime
optionNotes: Correct. Distributed Connect keeps connector configs, source offsets, and statuses in three internal topics, so any worker can take over any task. Parallelism comes from tasks: tasks.max lets the connector partition its work (tables, topic partitions) into up to that many tasks balanced across workers.
No shared filesystem is involved; state lives in Kafka itself.
Connect does not use ZooKeeper for its state.
Task counts can be changed by reconfiguring the connector at runtime via the REST API.
questions.explanation: Distributed Connect is a stateless worker cluster whose shared memory is Kafka: config/offset/status topics make workers interchangeable and failover automatic. tasks.max is the parallelism dial, subject to how far the specific connector can actually split its workload.

questions.q: Each record flowing through a sink connector needs a field removed and the event routed to a topic-per-region before delivery. The team debates implementing this inside Connect. What is the appropriate guidance?
options: Small per-record edits are what SMTs are for; heavier joins/aggregations belong in a stream processor
Connect cannot modify records at all; a Streams app must rewrite the data first
Fork the connector's source code and add the logic to its put() method
SMTs fully support aggregations and joins, so essentially all processing can move into the connector
optionNotes: Correct. SMTs are stateless, per-record transformations chained in connector config (e.g. ReplaceField to drop a field, RegexRouter/TimestampRouter to redirect topics) — a perfect fit here. They deliberately cannot hold state, so multi-record logic belongs in Kafka Streams/ksqlDB upstream.
SMTs exist precisely to modify records in the Connect pipeline.
Forking a connector for config-expressible logic creates a maintenance burden SMTs avoid.
SMTs see one record at a time; stateful operations are out of their scope by design.
questions.explanation: The dividing line: SMT for stateless single-record tweaks declared in configuration; stream processing for anything stateful or multi-record. Keeping that boundary keeps connectors reusable and pipelines debuggable.

questions.q: A team wants fast unit tests for a Kafka Streams topology — verifying that given input records, the expected output records and state are produced — without starting any broker. Which tool is designed for this?
options: TopologyTestDriver with TestInputTopic and TestOutputTopic
A production-like three-broker cluster running in a dedicated staging environment
MockProducer from kafka-clients
kafka-console-consumer run in a shell script
optionNotes: Correct. TopologyTestDriver executes the topology synchronously in-process: pipe records in via TestInputTopic, read results from TestOutputTopic, inspect state stores, and even advance the mocked wall clock for windowing tests — milliseconds per test, no cluster.
Real clusters are for integration tests; they are far too slow and flaky to be the unit-test loop.
MockProducer stubs the plain producer client; it knows nothing about Streams topologies.
Console tools are for manual inspection, not automated assertions.
questions.explanation: TopologyTestDriver is the Streams unit-testing harness: deterministic, in-JVM, broker-free, with control over event time and access to state stores. Save real brokers (Testcontainers/embedded) for integration coverage.

questions.q: A service class wraps a KafkaProducer and adds business logic before sending. How can its behaviour — including how it handles send failures — be unit-tested without a broker?
options: Inject a MockProducer, which records sent records for assertions and can be told to complete sends successfully or with an exception
Point the real producer at localhost and assert nothing is thrown
Use reflection to verify the producer's private buffer
Skip unit tests entirely; only full integration tests can meaningfully cover producer code
optionNotes: Correct. MockProducer implements the Producer interface in memory: tests assert on history() to check what was sent (topic, key, value) and use errorNext(exception) to simulate broker failures, exercising the error-handling paths deterministically.
A real client with no broker just fails on metadata — it proves nothing about the logic and makes tests environment-dependent.
Reflection against internals is brittle and tests the client library, not your code.
The clients ship mock implementations precisely so this code can be unit-tested.
questions.explanation: Program against the Producer/Consumer interfaces and inject MockProducer/MockConsumer in tests: sends are captured for assertion and failures are scriptable — fast, deterministic coverage of both happy and error paths.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does this output indicate, and which metric quantifies it?
questions.exhibit:
$ kafka-consumer-groups --bootstrap-server broker:9092 \
    --describe --group invoice-processor

TOPIC     PARTITION  CURRENT-OFFSET  LOG-END-OFFSET  LAG
invoices  0          182300          195450          13150
invoices  1          182410          195600          13190
invoices  2          182150          195510          13360
options: The group is falling behind — ~13k records of lag per partition; watch records-lag-max
The brokers are dropping records under load; the metric to watch is under-replicated-partitions
The producers are throttled; the metric is produce-throttle-time
The topic is over-partitioned; the metric is partition-count
optionNotes: Correct. LAG = log-end-offset − committed offset: the number of records waiting per partition. Growing lag means consumption is slower than production — the primary consumer health signal, exposed as records-lag/records-lag-max on the consumer's fetch metrics (and by external lag monitors).
Nothing here concerns replication; records are safely stored and simply not yet consumed.
Producer throttling is unrelated to a consumer group's committed offsets.
Even lag across three partitions says nothing about having too many partitions.
questions.explanation: Consumer lag is the queue depth of Kafka consumption. Watching its trend (per partition, via kafka-consumer-groups, JMX records-lag-max, or dedicated exporters) distinguishes a healthy steady state from a group that will never catch up and needs scaling or faster processing.

questions.q: After a deployment, invoices intermittently fail to appear downstream, yet application logs show no errors: the producer code ignores send outcomes. Which producer-side JMX metrics would have revealed the problem, and what code change makes failures visible?
options: record-error-rate / record-retry-rate rising above zero; add a send callback to log failures
consumer records-lag-max; switch the consumer to read_committed
under-min-isr-partition-count climbing on the broker; the fix is to raise the replication factor
request-latency-avg; enable compression to lower it
optionNotes: Correct. The producer exposes record-error-rate (records that ultimately failed) and record-retry-rate (transient failures being retried). Fire-and-forget send() without a callback silently drops those errors on the floor — the callback (metadata, exception) -> {...} is where failures must be logged, alerted, or routed for retry.
Consumer lag cannot detect records that were never successfully produced.
Broker ISR health is one possible cause, but the observability gap described is on the producer, and the records may fail for many other reasons.
Latency metrics do not surface silent record loss.
questions.explanation: A producer that ignores send results is blind to its own failures. record-error-rate is the canary in monitoring, and the completion callback is the code-level fix — every production producer should have both.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does this producer configuration guarantee, and what does it NOT guarantee?
options: No duplicates from retries and no reordering within a partition; NOT end-to-end exactly-once across topics
Exactly-once delivery all the way to the downstream consumers with no further configuration required
That every produced record is delivered to consumers in strict global order across all partitions
Nothing useful, because idempotence has no effect unless transactional.id is also configured on it
optionNotes: Correct — idempotence + acks=all dedupes retries and preserves per-partition order, but EOS across topics needs transactions.
Consumer-side exactly-once needs transactions and read_committed, not just an idempotent producer.
Ordering is per partition only; there is no global cross-partition order.
Idempotence works without transactional.id; the transactional.id is only needed for transactions.
questions.explanation: An idempotent producer (acks=all) removes retry duplicates and preserves per-partition order, but end-to-end exactly-once across topics requires transactions and read_committed consumers.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit. What does this sink-connector configuration achieve for malformed records?
options: Bad records are skipped and routed to dlq-orders with context headers, so the task keeps running
The connector stops its task on the very first malformed record it encounters in the stream
Malformed records are silently dropped with no way to inspect or recover them afterward
Every record, valid or not, is copied into dlq-orders in addition to the target system
optionNotes: Correct — tolerate errors and send failed records (with context headers) to the DLQ topic while the task continues.
That is errors.tolerance=none behavior, the opposite of this config.
The DLQ is exactly what makes the bad records inspectable, not silently dropped.
Only failed records go to the DLQ, not every record.
questions.explanation: errors.tolerance=all plus a dead-letter-queue topic lets a sink connector skip malformed records (routing them, with context headers, to the DLQ) while the task keeps running.

questions.q: Refer to the exhibit (kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe). What does this row indicate?
questions.exhibit:
GROUP       TOPIC     PARTITION  CURRENT-OFFSET  LOG-END-OFFSET  LAG
billing     payments  3          48120           61240           13120
options: The consumer is 13120 records behind on partition 3 — lag to monitor and alert on
The topic partition 3 holds exactly 48120 records in total at this moment
The consumer has read 13120 records ahead of the end of the log somehow
The partition has 61240 consumers, one per record, which is far too many
optionNotes: Correct — lag = log-end (61240) − current (48120) = 13120 records behind on that partition.
Current-offset is a position, not the total record count of the partition.
A consumer cannot read past the log-end offset.
Those numbers are offsets, not consumer counts.
questions.explanation: Consumer lag = log-end offset − current offset for a partition; here 61240 − 48120 = 13120 records behind, the key signal to monitor and alert on.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Fundamentals
objectives: Explain Kafka's data model: topics, partitions, offsets, and ordering
Describe brokers, replication, leaders/followers, and the ISR
Explain message keys, key hashing, and partition assignment
Describe consumer groups and how partitions are distributed
Explain data retention and log compaction
Compare delivery semantics: at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once
Describe Kafka security fundamentals (encryption, authentication, authorization)
sections.notes:
Topics, partitions, and offsets

  • A topic is a named stream of records, split into partitions. Each partition is an ordered, immutable, append-only log; each record in it gets a sequential offset.
  • Ordering is guaranteed only WITHIN a partition — never across partitions of a topic. If a use case needs total order for a set of records (e.g. all events of one customer), those records must share a partition (⇒ same key).
  • Consumers track their position as the next offset to read per partition; reading does NOT remove data (Kafka is a log, not a queue) — many independent consumer groups read the same data.
  • Partition count = the topic's maximum parallelism for a consumer group. Partitions can be added later (breaks key→partition mapping for existing keys!) but never removed.
  • Records are key/value pairs plus a timestamp and optional headers (metadata for tracing, routing, schema hints — not used for partitioning).

Brokers, replication, and the ISR

  • A cluster is a set of brokers; each partition lives on brokers as one leader replica plus replication.factor − 1 follower replicas. All produce/consume traffic goes through the leader (followers replicate by fetching).
  • The ISR (in-sync replicas) is the set of replicas fully caught up with the leader. On leader failure, a new leader is elected from the ISR — no data loss if the ISR had the data.
  • min.insync.replicas (broker/topic config): the minimum ISR size for writes with acks=all to succeed. The classic durable setup: RF=3, min.insync.replicas=2, acks=all — tolerates one broker down while never acknowledging a write held by fewer than 2 replicas; producers get NotEnoughReplicasException if the ISR shrinks below 2.
  • Unclean leader election (electing a non-ISR replica) trades data loss for availability; it is disabled by default.

Message keys and partition assignment

  • Producer partitioning: key present → partition = murmur2(key) % numPartitions — the same key ALWAYS lands on the same partition (ordering per key). Key = null → sticky partitioning: the producer fills a batch to one random partition, then switches (efficient round-robin-ish distribution).
  • A producer can also set the partition explicitly or plug a custom Partitioner.
  • Design consequence: choose keys with enough cardinality to spread load; a low-cardinality key (e.g. country) creates hot partitions. Changing partition count remaps keys — plan partitions up front for keyed topics.

Consumer groups

  • Consumers with the same group.id form a consumer group: each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. More consumers than partitions ⇒ the extras sit idle.
  • Different groups are independent — each receives every record (pub-sub across groups, queue-like within a group).
  • Group membership is coordinated by a broker acting as group coordinator; consumers heartbeat, and joins/leaves/failures trigger a rebalance (partition reassignment). Committed offsets are stored in the internal __consumer_offsets topic.

Retention and log compaction

Cleanup policyBehaviorUse case
delete (default)Segments older than retention.ms (default 7 days) or beyond retention.bytes are deleted — regardless of consumptionEvent streams, telemetry
compactKeeps at least the latest record per key; older values for the same key are eventually removed; a null value ("tombstone") deletes the keyChangelogs, table-like state (latest value per entity)
compact,deleteBoth: compaction plus time/size-based expiryBounded changelogs

Retention is per topic; consumers must keep up or re-read from what still exists (auto.offset.reset decides where a group starts with no valid offset: earliest or latest).

Delivery semantics

SemanticsHow it happensCost
At-most-onceCommit/ack BEFORE processing (or producer with acks=0) — failures lose data, never duplicateData loss possible
At-least-onceProcess THEN commit; producer retries — failures re-deliverDuplicates possible ⇒ consumers should be idempotent
Exactly-onceIdempotent producer + transactions (Kafka-to-Kafka), consumer isolation.level=read_committedSome latency/throughput overhead; EOS applies to Kafka↔Kafka pipelines (external sinks still need idempotency)

Kafka security basics

  • Encryption in transit: TLS between clients and brokers (and inter-broker); listeners defined per protocol (PLAINTEXT, SSL, SASL_SSL…).
  • Authentication: SASL mechanisms — PLAIN (username/password, over TLS!), SCRAM-SHA-256/512, GSSAPI (Kerberos), OAUTHBEARER — or mutual TLS (client certificates).
  • Authorization: ACLs per principal on resources (topic, group, cluster) and operations (Read, Write, Create…). A typical client needs Read on the topic + Read on its consumer group, or Write on the topic to produce.
  • Clients configure this with security.protocol, sasl.mechanism, sasl.jaas.config, plus truststore/keystore settings.

KRaft and the control plane

Kafka's metadata (topics, partitions, leaders, configs) was historically coordinated by ZooKeeper; modern Kafka uses KRaft — brokers/controllers run the Raft consensus protocol internally, removing ZooKeeper entirely (default since Kafka 4.0). For a developer the client APIs are unchanged; know that cluster metadata and controller election are internal (a controller quorum) and that administration happens via the Admin API/CLI tools.

Exam tip: the most-tested fundamentals: ordering only within a partition; same key → same partition; consumers > partitions ⇒ idle consumers; RF=3 + min.insync.replicas=2 + acks=all tolerates exactly one broker loss; compaction keeps the latest value per key (tombstones delete). Memorize these cold.

Partitions map to consumers in a group

A consumer group divides a topic's partitions among its members, so the maximum useful parallelism equals the partition count. Any consumer beyond that sits idle; a different group.id gets its own full copy of the stream.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Application Development
objectives: Configure and tune producers (acks, batching, retries, idempotence)
Implement exactly-once semantics with idempotent producers and transactions
Configure and tune consumers (poll loop, offset commits, rebalancing)
Use serializers/deserializers and Schema Registry with Avro/JSON/Protobuf
Handle retriable and non-retriable errors in clients
Use the Admin API and command-line tools
Apply compression and batching for throughput
sections.notes:
The producer: send path and key configs

send() is asynchronous: records go through the (key/value) serializer → partitioner → an in-memory batch per partition (buffer.memory), and a background I/O thread ships batches. A callback (or the returned Future) reports success (RecordMetadata) or failure.

ConfigMeaning
acks0 = fire-and-forget; 1 = leader has written it; all = all in-sync replicas have it (durable; pair with min.insync.replicas)
retries / delivery.timeout.msAutomatic retry of transient failures; delivery.timeout.ms bounds the whole attempt
enable.idempotenceBroker de-duplicates producer retries (sequence numbers) — no duplicates from retries, per-partition ordering preserved; default true in modern clients
max.in.flight.requests.per.connectionConcurrent unacked requests; with idempotence ≤5 ordering is still guaranteed
linger.ms / batch.sizeWait up to linger.ms to fill batches up to batch.size — the main throughput/latency trade-off knobs
compression.typenone / gzip / snappy / lz4 / zstd — compresses whole batches

Idempotence and transactions (EOS)

  • Idempotent producer = no duplicates from retries on one producer session. It does NOT dedupe application-level re-sends.
  • Transactions: set a stable transactional.id, then initTransactions() → beginTransaction() → send(...) + sendOffsetsToTransaction(...) → commitTransaction(). Enables atomic consume-transform-produce: output records and consumed-offset commits succeed or abort together.
  • Downstream consumers must set isolation.level=read_committed to skip aborted records (default is read_uncommitted).
  • Exactly-once applies Kafka-to-Kafka; writing to an external DB still needs idempotent writes or its own transaction coordination.

The consumer: poll loop, commits, rebalancing

while (true) {
  ConsumerRecords<K,V> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(250));
  for (ConsumerRecord<K,V> rec : records) process(rec);
  consumer.commitSync();          // manual at-least-once
}
  • Offset commits: enable.auto.commit=true commits every auto.commit.interval.ms during poll (risk: commits records not yet processed → possible loss on crash). Manual commitSync() (blocking, retried) or commitAsync() (fast, no retry) AFTER processing gives at-least-once.
  • Liveness: heartbeats (background thread) within session.timeout.ms keep membership; max.poll.interval.ms caps time between polls — exceed it (slow processing) and the group evicts the consumer, causing a rebalance. Tune it, or lower max.poll.records.
  • Rebalancing: assignment strategies include range, round-robin, sticky, and cooperative-sticky (incremental — only moved partitions stop, no global stop-the-world). A ConsumerRebalanceListener lets you commit/cleanup on revocation.
  • auto.offset.reset (earliest/latest/none) applies only when the group has NO valid committed offset.
  • Consumers are not thread-safe — one consumer per thread; scale by adding consumers (up to partition count).

Serialization and Schema Registry

  • Producers serialize keys/values to bytes; consumers must deserialize with a compatible scheme. Common formats: Avro, JSON Schema, Protobuf (all supported by Confluent SerDes) — plus primitives (String, Long…).
  • Schema Registry stores versioned schemas per subject (default: <topic>-key / <topic>-value); the wire format is a magic byte + schema ID + payload — consumers fetch schemas by ID and cache them.
  • Compatibility modes govern schema evolution:
ModeNew schema can be used to read…Safe changesUpgrade order
BACKWARD (default)data written with the previous schemadelete fields, add optional fields (with defaults)Consumers first
FORWARDold schema reads data written with the new oneadd fields, delete optional fieldsProducers first
FULLboth directionsonly add/remove optional fields with defaultsAny order

*_TRANSITIVE variants check against ALL previous versions, not just the latest.

Error handling: retriable vs non-retriable

Retriable (client retries automatically)Non-retriable (fix required)
LeaderNotAvailable / NotLeaderForPartition, NetworkException, NotEnoughReplicas, timeouts — transient cluster eventsSerializationException, RecordTooLargeException (> max.request.size / message.max.bytes), AuthorizationException, InvalidTopicException

Producer: rely on retries + idempotence for the left column; catch and handle (log/DLQ/alert) the right. Consumer: a poison record that fails deserialization will fail on EVERY poll — catch it, skip past it (seek beyond, or use an error-handling deserializer) and route it to a dead-letter topic.

Admin API and CLI tools

  • AdminClient: programmatic create/describe/delete topics, alter configs, list consumer groups and their offsets/lag, manage ACLs.
  • CLI equivalents: kafka-topics.sh (--create --partitions --replication-factor / --describe), kafka-console-producer.sh / kafka-console-consumer.sh (--from-beginning, --group), kafka-consumer-groups.sh (--describe shows per-partition LAG; --reset-offsets), kafka-configs.sh.

Compression and batching

  • Compression is set on the producer (whole-batch compression); brokers usually store batches as-is; consumers decompress. Bigger batches (higher linger.ms/batch.size) compress better — compounding throughput gains at a small latency cost.
  • zstd/lz4 are the modern favorites (good ratio/CPU balance); gzip best ratio, most CPU.

Exam tip: favorite questions — what acks=all requires (paired min.insync.replicas); idempotence stopping retry duplicates but transactions being needed for consume-transform-produce EOS; auto-commit's data-loss window vs manual commit-after-processing; max.poll.interval.ms evictions on slow processing; BACKWARD compatibility = consumers upgrade first; and the retriable/non-retriable split.

Delivery semantics at a glance

The guarantee you get is a function of producer and consumer configuration. Pick the row the requirement demands — stronger guarantees cost a little latency and coordination.

sections.title: Apache Kafka Streams
objectives: Describe the Streams processing topology, tasks, and threading model
Choose between KStream, KTable, and GlobalKTable
Use stateful operations: aggregations, state stores, and internal topics
Apply windowing (tumbling, hopping, sliding, session) and time semantics
Join streams and tables correctly
Configure processing guarantees (at-least-once, exactly-once)
sections.notes:
Topology, tasks, and threads

  • Kafka Streams is a client library (plain Java app — no cluster to install; deploy like any app, e.g. in Docker/Kubernetes). A topology is the graph of sources → processors → sinks built with the DSL (StreamsBuilder) or Processor API.
  • The topology is split into tasks — one per input partition — the unit of parallelism. Tasks are distributed over num.stream.threads and over all app instances sharing the same application.id (which is also the consumer group id). Max parallelism = input partition count; extra instances/threads idle.
  • Scaling out = start another instance with the same application.id: tasks (and their state) migrate automatically.

KStream, KTable, GlobalKTable

KStreamKTableGlobalKTable
SemanticsEvent stream — every record is an independent fact ("orders placed")Changelog — latest value per key ("current price per product"); updates overwriteLike KTable but FULLY replicated to every instance
Reads topicAll partitions split across tasksAll partitions split across tasksEvery instance reads ALL partitions
Join keyingStream-stream and stream-table joins require co-partitioning (same key, same partition count)Join on any key (via mapper) — no co-partitioning needed
Typical useTransformations, enrichment eventsAggregation results, reference data that updatesSmall, broadly needed lookup data

Stateful processing: state stores and internal topics

  • Stateful operations (count, aggregate, reduce, joins, windowing) keep local state stores (RocksDB on disk by default, in-memory optional).
  • Every state store is backed by a compacted changelog topic in Kafka — the source of truth. On instance failure/migration, state is restored by replaying the changelog; num.standby.replicas keeps warm copies to shorten failover.
  • Re-keying operations (groupBy, selectKey before an aggregation/join) create internal repartition topics so records with the same new key meet in the same task.
  • State is queryable in-process (interactive queries) for serving lookups without an external DB.

Windowing and time

WindowShapeExample
TumblingFixed size, non-overlappingCount per 5 minutes
HoppingFixed size, overlapping (size + advance)5-min window advancing every 1 min
SlidingBound to record pairs within a time differenceJoins/aggregations over "within 30 s of each other"
SessionActivity-based, closed by an inactivity gapUser sessions with 10-min idle gap
  • Streams operates on event time by default (record timestamps; custom TimestampExtractor possible), not wall-clock arrival time.
  • Late/out-of-order records within the window's grace period still update it; results are continuously refined (use suppress() to emit only the final result when a window closes).

Joins

  • Stream–stream joins are always windowed (records must occur within the window) and support inner/left/outer.
  • Stream–table joins enrich events with the table's current value for the key (inner/left).
  • Table–table joins produce an updating joined table.
  • Except with GlobalKTables, join inputs must be co-partitioned: same key space and the SAME number of partitions — otherwise Streams throws or requires an explicit repartition.

Processing guarantees

  • processing.guarantee=at_least_once (default) — duplicates possible after failures.
  • processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 — wraps the consume-process-produce cycle (including state-store changelogs) in Kafka transactions; requires brokers ≥ 2.5, adds modest overhead. This is the one-line EOS switch for Streams apps.

Exam tip: classic questions — tasks = input partitions (adding instances beyond that does nothing); KTable = latest value per key (vs KStream = every event); GlobalKTable = fully replicated, no co-partitioning; state restored from changelog topics; exactly_once_v2 as the EOS setting; tumbling vs hopping vs session recognition from a scenario.

A topology and its internal topics

Re-keying (groupBy/selectKey) inserts a repartition topic; stateful operators (count/aggregate/reduce) back their state store with a changelog topic for fault-tolerant restore.

sections.title: Kafka Connect
objectives: Explain Kafka Connect's purpose: source and sink integration without custom code
Compare standalone and distributed worker deployments
Configure connectors and scale them with tasks
Choose converters and integrate Schema Registry
Apply Single Message Transforms
Configure error tolerance and dead letter queues
Describe CDC (change data capture) with source connectors
sections.notes:
What Connect is and when to use it

  • Kafka Connect is the framework for configuration-driven integration between Kafka and external systems: source connectors pull data INTO Kafka; sink connectors push data OUT of Kafka. No custom producer/consumer code — you deploy a connector plugin and give it a JSON/properties config.
  • Connect handles the hard parts for you: offset tracking (where the source left off / what the sink has written), scaling, fault tolerance, retries, schemas, and transformation hooks. Prefer it over hand-written glue code whenever a connector exists.

Workers: standalone vs distributed

StandaloneDistributed
ProcessesOne worker processCluster of workers sharing a group.id
Config/offsets storedLocal filesInternal Kafka topics (config, offsets, status)
Fault tolerance / scalingNone — the process IS the systemWork is rebalanced across workers; add workers to scale
ManagementProperties file at startupREST API (create/pause/resume/delete connectors, view status)
UseDev, testing, one-off local jobsAnything production

Connectors, tasks, and scaling

  • A connector instance is the logical job (e.g. "this database → these topics"); it divides work into tasks — the actual units that copy data, spread across workers.
  • tasks.max caps parallelism; the effective task count also depends on the source's dividable units (e.g. number of tables, or a JDBC source may cap at one task) and, for sinks, the topic's partitions.
  • Failed tasks can be restarted via the REST API (/connectors/<name>/status, /restart).

Converters and Schema Registry

  • Converters translate between Connect's internal data representation and the bytes in Kafka: key.converter / value.converter set per worker or per connector. Common: AvroConverter, ProtobufConverter, JsonSchemaConverter (all with ...converter.schema.registry.url), plain JsonConverter (optionally with embedded schemas), StringConverter.
  • Converters are chosen INDEPENDENTLY of the connector — the same JDBC source can write Avro or JSON depending on converter config. Mismatched converters between producer side and consumer side are the classic "deserialization garbage" root cause.

Single Message Transforms (SMTs)

  • Lightweight, per-record transformations applied in the connector pipeline (source: before writing to Kafka; sink: before writing to the target): rename/mask/insert fields, route records to different topics, cast types, extract keys, filter records.
  • Configured as a chain: transforms=mask,route with per-transform classes/settings. For anything heavier (joins, aggregations, lookups) use a stream processor (Kafka Streams) between source and sink instead of stretching SMTs.

Error handling and dead letter queues

  • errors.tolerance=none (default — task fails on a bad record) vs all (skip and continue).
  • Sink connectors can route failed records to a dead letter queue topic: errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (+ context.headers.enable to attach failure reasons as headers). DLQs apply to sinks, not sources.
  • errors.retry.timeout enables retries of transient failures before tolerance/DLQ handling kicks in.

Change Data Capture (CDC)

  • CDC source connectors (e.g. Debezium) read a database's transaction log (not query polling) and publish every row-level INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE as an event — low latency, no load on tables, captures deletes.
  • Contrast with JDBC source polling (periodic SELECTs with an incrementing/timestamp column): simpler, but misses deletes, adds DB load, and has poll-interval latency.
  • CDC events conventionally carry before/after images; deletes are followed by tombstones for compacted topics — pairing naturally with KTable-style consumption.

Exam tip: distributed workers store config/offsets/status in internal Kafka topics and are managed over REST — standalone uses local files; that contrast is heavily tested. So are: tasks as the unit of parallelism (tasks.max), converters being independent of connectors, DLQs being a SINK feature, and log-based CDC vs polling trade-offs.

sections.title: Application Testing
objectives: Unit test producer and consumer logic with mocks
Test Kafka Streams topologies with TopologyTestDriver
Design integration tests against real (containerized/embedded) brokers
Test schema compatibility before deployment
sections.notes:
Unit testing producers and consumers

  • The Kafka clients library ships test doubles: MockProducer and MockConsumer — no broker needed.
  • MockProducer records everything your code sends (history()) and can simulate success or failure of sends (errorNext(exception)) — assert what was produced, to which topic/partition, with which key, and that callbacks/error paths behave.
  • MockConsumer lets tests seed assigned partitions, records, and end offsets, then verifies the poll-loop logic: processing, commit behavior, rebalance listener handling.
  • Structure application code so the Kafka client is injected — logic classes take a Producer<K,V>/Consumer<K,V> interface, tests pass the mock, production passes the real client.

Testing Kafka Streams topologies

TopologyTestDriver driver = new TopologyTestDriver(builder.build(), props);
TestInputTopic<String, Order> in =
    driver.createInputTopic("orders", stringSerde.serializer(), orderSerde.serializer());
TestOutputTopic<String, Long> out =
    driver.createOutputTopic("order-counts", stringSerde.deserializer(), longSerde.deserializer());

in.pipeInput("cust-1", order1);
in.pipeInput("cust-1", order2);
assertEquals(2L, out.readKeyValue().value);   // synchronous, deterministic
  • TopologyTestDriver executes the topology synchronously without any broker: pipe records into TestInputTopics, read results from TestOutputTopics, and query state stores directly (driver.getKeyValueStore(...)).
  • It controls time explicitly — advance wall-clock/event time to test windowing, grace periods, and suppression deterministically (no sleeps, no flakiness).

Integration testing with real brokers

  • Unit tests can't cover: real serialization against a Schema Registry, security handshakes, rebalancing behavior, transactions, connector configs. For those, run a REAL Kafka in the test: Testcontainers (Kafka container per test class — today's standard), embedded Kafka utilities, or a shared test cluster.
  • Keep them separate from unit tests (slower, resource-heavy); make topics unique per run; assert with consumers polling until a timeout rather than fixed sleeps.
  • Test failure modes deliberately: kill the container to observe retry/idempotence behavior; produce poison records to verify DLQ paths.

Testing schema evolution

  • Before deploying a producer with a changed schema, verify compatibility against the registry: the Schema Registry compatibility check REST endpoint (or Maven plugin schema-registry:test-compatibility) validates a candidate schema against the subject's configured mode (BACKWARD/FORWARD/FULL) — wire this into CI so incompatible changes fail the build, not production consumers.

Exam tip: map tool to layer — MockProducer/MockConsumer for client logic, TopologyTestDriver (with TestInputTopic/TestOutputTopic) for Streams topologies without a broker, Testcontainers/embedded Kafka for integration concerns, and registry compatibility checks in CI for schema changes. TopologyTestDriver's deterministic time control for window testing is a favorite question.

sections.title: Application Observability
objectives: Expose and collect client metrics (JMX) for producers, consumers, and Streams
Monitor consumer lag and alert on it
Track producer health: errors, retries, latency, throughput
Use logging, tracing, and interceptors for end-to-end visibility
Troubleshoot common client-side problems from metrics
sections.notes:
Client metrics via JMX

  • All Kafka clients (producer, consumer, Streams, Connect) expose metrics through JMX MBeans; scrape them with your monitoring stack (Prometheus JMX exporter, etc.). Metrics exist per client, per topic, and per node.
  • Kafka Streams adds thread/task/processor/state-store metrics (metrics.recording.level controls granularity); Connect exposes connector/task status and throughput metrics.
  • Watch BOTH sides: client metrics tell you how YOUR app behaves; broker-side metrics (under-replicated partitions, request handler idle %) tell you about the cluster — a developer is expected to read the client side fluently.

Consumer lag — the number-one signal

  • Lag = log end offset − committed/current offset, per partition: how far behind the consumer is. Growing lag means the app can't keep up (or stopped).
  • Sources: consumer metric records-lag-max, kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group app (shows CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET, LAG per partition), and external lag exporters (e.g. Burrow) for fleet-wide monitoring.
  • Alert on sustained growth, not momentary spikes. Remedies: faster processing (batch/parallelize the work), more consumers (up to partition count), more partitions (planned!), or tuning max.poll.records/processing pipeline.
  • Also watch rebalance metrics (rebalance rate, time-between-poll) — frequent rebalances often masquerade as lag problems.

Producer health metrics

MetricWhat it tells you
record-error-rateSends that ultimately FAILED (post-retries) — should be ~0; alert otherwise
record-retry-rateTransient trouble being retried — early warning of cluster/network issues
request-latency-avg/maxBroker round-trip time — rising latency precedes buffer exhaustion
batch-size-avg, records-per-request-avg, compression-rate-avgBatching efficiency — small batches = tune linger.ms/batch.size
buffer-available-bytes / bufferpool-wait-timebuffer.memory pressure — send() will start blocking (max.block.ms) when exhausted

Logging, tracing, and interceptors

  • Client logs (log4j/slf4j) surface warnings you should not ignore: rebalances, disconnections, expired transactions, slow commits.
  • Message headers carry correlation/trace IDs across the pipeline; standard practice is W3C/OpenTelemetry trace context propagated producer → consumer so one business transaction is traceable end to end.
  • Interceptors (ProducerInterceptor / ConsumerInterceptor) hook onSend/onConsume for cross-cutting telemetry (count, tag, trace) without touching business code — this is how Confluent Monitoring interceptors work.
  • End-to-end latency = event timestamp vs processing time at the consumer — measure it; per-hop metrics alone hide pipeline delays.

Troubleshooting common scenarios

SymptomLikely cause → check
Lag grows on ALL partitionsProcessing too slow overall → processing time per record, thread pool, downstream calls
Lag grows on ONE partitionHot key/skewed partitioning, or one stuck consumer → key distribution, that consumer's logs
Consumer keeps leaving the grouppoll() gaps exceed max.poll.interval.ms → reduce max.poll.records or speed up processing
Duplicates downstreamAt-least-once + rebalances/crashes between processing and commit → idempotent handling or EOS/transactions
Producer TimeoutException, buffer fullCluster slow/unreachable or produce rate > capacity → request latency, cluster health, buffer.memory
Consumer gets garbage / DeserializationExceptionSerializer/converter mismatch or schema change → producer serializer vs consumer deserializer, registry compatibility

Exam tip: consumer LAG is the metric the exam cares most about — know its definition, where to read it (metrics + kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe), and the remediation options. Producer-side, record-error-rate vs record-retry-rate (failed vs retried) is the classic pair. And every "consumer evicted from group" scenario resolves to max.poll.interval.ms.

What consumer lag measures

Lag is how far a group trails the head of the log on a partition: log-end-offset − current(committed)-offset. Growing lag means the consumer can't keep up with the producer.

cards.back: Ordered ONLY within a partition. Same key → same partition (murmur2(key) % numPartitions) → per-key ordering.

cards.front: Consumer group with more consumers than partitions?
cards.back: Extras sit IDLE — each partition goes to exactly one consumer per group. Partition count = max parallelism.
replication.factor=3, min.insync.replicas=2, producer acks=all — tolerates one broker down; every ack'd write on ≥2 replicas; NotEnoughReplicas if ISR < 2.
In-Sync Replicas: replicas fully caught up with the leader. New leaders are elected FROM the ISR (no data loss). Unclean leader election (non-ISR) is off by default.
cleanup.policy=compact keeps at least the LATEST value per key (changelog). A null-value record (tombstone) deletes the key after tombstone retention.
7 days (retention.ms) or retention.bytes — deletion regardless of consumption; Kafka is a log, not a queue.

cards.front: Adding partitions to a keyed topic — consequence?
cards.back: hash % numPartitions changes → keys REMAP; per-key ordering breaks across the change. Partitions can never be removed.

cards.front: ACLs needed to consume topic T with group G?
cards.back: READ on topic T AND READ on group G (offset commits are group operations).
KRaft — an internal Raft controller quorum manages cluster metadata (default since Kafka 4.0). Client APIs unchanged.
0: fire-and-forget. 1: leader wrote it (lost if leader dies pre-replication). all: all in-sync replicas have it (pair with min.insync.replicas).
Duplicates from the producer's own RETRIES (broker dedupes by sequence number), preserving per-partition order. NOT app-level re-sends, NOT consumer duplicates.
Transactions: transactional.id + initTransactions + beginTransaction + sendOffsetsToTransaction + commitTransaction; consumers read with isolation.level=read_committed.
linger.ms (wait to fill batches) + batch.size + compression.type (whole-batch: zstd/lz4/snappy/gzip) — bigger batches compress and ship better.
enable.auto.commit can commit offsets for records NOT yet processed → crash loses them. At-least-once = process first, then commitSync/commitAsync.

cards.front: Consumer keeps getting kicked from the group during slow processing — which setting?
cards.back: max.poll.interval.ms exceeded (time between poll() calls; default 5 min). Raise it or reduce max.poll.records. Heartbeats (session.timeout.ms) run on a background thread.
ONLY when the group has no valid committed offset (new group, expired, out-of-range). earliest/latest/none.
Magic byte + 4-byte SCHEMA ID + payload. Consumers fetch the writer's schema by ID from Schema Registry (cached).

cards.front: BACKWARD vs FORWARD compatibility — who upgrades first?
cards.back: BACKWARD (default): new schema reads OLD data → CONSUMERS first. FORWARD: old schema reads NEW data → PRODUCERS first. FULL: both.

cards.front: Retriable vs non-retriable producer errors — examples?
cards.back: Retriable: NotLeaderForPartition, NetworkException, NotEnoughReplicas (transient). Non-retriable: SerializationException, RecordTooLargeException, AuthorizationException.

cards.front: CLI to see a group's lag per partition?
cards.back: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group <name> → CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET, LAG (also --reset-offsets).
One TASK per input partition; tasks spread across threads/instances sharing application.id (= consumer group id). Instances beyond partition count idle.
KStream: every record an event. KTable: latest value per key (changelog). GlobalKTable: fully replicated to each instance; joins need NO co-partitioning.

cards.front: What backs a state store for fault tolerance?
cards.back: A compacted CHANGELOG topic; state is restored by replay; num.standby.replicas keeps warm copies for fast failover.
Tumbling: fixed, no overlap. Hopping: fixed size + smaller advance (overlap). Sliding: pairs within a time difference. Session: closed by inactivity gap.
processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 (wraps consume-process-produce + changelogs in transactions; brokers ≥ 2.5).

cards.front: Emit only the FINAL result per window?
cards.back: suppress(untilWindowCloses) — buffers intermediate updates until the window + grace period end.

cards.front: Connect distributed mode: where is state stored, how is it managed?
cards.back: Config/offsets/status in internal KAFKA topics; managed via the REST API; tasks rebalance across workers on failure. Standalone = local files, no fault tolerance.
Connector = logical job definition; TASKS are the parallel data-copying units (bounded by tasks.max and partitions/tables).

cards.front: Converters — role and the classic mistake?
cards.back: key/value.converter translate Connect records ↔ bytes (Avro/JSON Schema/Protobuf need schema.registry.url). Independent of the connector. Mismatch with the topic's actual format = garbage/deserialization errors.

cards.front: Connect error tolerance + DLQ — configs and limitation?
cards.back: errors.tolerance=all + errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (+context.headers.enable). DLQs are a SINK connector feature.
CDC (Debezium) reads the DB transaction log: captures DELETEs, low latency, no table load. Polling misses deletes, loads the DB, latency = poll interval.

cards.front: Test tools by layer: client logic / Streams topology / real-broker concerns?
cards.back: MockProducer & MockConsumer / TopologyTestDriver (TestInputTopic, TestOutputTopic, state stores, time control) / Testcontainers or embedded Kafka.

cards.front: Prevent schema changes breaking consumers — where?
cards.back: In CI: validate candidate schemas against the registry's compatibility mode (REST check or schema-registry:test-compatibility Maven goal).
LOG-END-OFFSET − committed/current offset per partition. Watch records-lag-max metric or kafka-consumer-groups.sh; alert on SUSTAINED growth.
error-rate = sends that FAILED after retries (data loss — alert on non-zero). retry-rate = transient trouble being retried (early warning).

cards.front: Cross-service tracing through Kafka — where does trace context travel?
cards.back: In message HEADERS (W3C/OpenTelemetry context), injected on produce and extracted on consume — never in the key (would change partitioning).

exercises.title: Create and inspect a topic with the CLI
exercises.task: Create a topic orders with 6 partitions and replication factor 3, then confirm the partition count, the leader/replica/ISR layout, and that the config took effect. Finally, increase it to 12 partitions and observe what changes.

given.label: Cluster
given.code: A running Kafka cluster (3 brokers), bootstrap at localhost:9092

steps: Create the topic with --partitions 6 --replication-factor 3
Describe it and read the Leader / Replicas / Isr columns per partition
Add partitions with --alter --partitions 12
Note: partitions can only be INCREASED, never decreased, and re-keys the key→partition mapping

solution.label: Commands
solution.code:
kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --create \
  --topic orders --partitions 6 --replication-factor 3

kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --describe --topic orders
# Topic: orders  PartitionCount: 6  ReplicationFactor: 3
# Partition: 0  Leader: 1  Replicas: 1,2,3  Isr: 1,2,3  ...

kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --alter \
  --topic orders --partitions 12   # increase only

exercises.solutionNotes: --describe shows, per partition, the leader broker (where produce/consume happens), the full replica set, and the ISR (in-sync replicas). Partition count can only ever be increased; adding partitions changes which partition a given key hashes to, so keyed ordering guarantees only hold for messages produced after the change.

exercises.title: Key-based ordering and consumer-group scaling
exercises.task: Prove two things with the console tools: (1) messages with the same key always land on the same partition (per-key ordering), and (2) within a consumer group, partitions are divided among consumers and an extra consumer beyond the partition count sits idle.
steps: Produce keyed records with parse.key=true and a key separator
Consume with --property print.partition=true to see key→partition stability
Start N consumers sharing --group g1 and watch partition assignment
Start a 7th consumer on 6 partitions and confirm it gets nothing

solution.label: Produce keyed, observe partition
solution.code:
kafka-console-producer --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --topic orders \
  --property parse.key=true --property key.separator=:
> cust-1:order A
> cust-1:order B      # same key → same partition, ordered
> cust-2:order C

kafka-console-consumer --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --topic orders \
  --from-beginning --property print.key=true --property print.partition=true

solution.label: Group scaling
solution.code:
# run in 3 terminals — they share the 6 partitions (2 each)
kafka-console-consumer --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --topic orders --group g1
# a 7th consumer in g1 stays idle: consumers > partitions ⇒ some idle

exercises.solutionNotes: Kafka orders records per partition, and a key is hashed to a fixed partition, so all records for one key are ordered. A consumer group splits partitions across its members for parallelism; the maximum useful parallelism equals the partition count, so any consumer beyond that is idle. Different group.ids each get a full independent copy of the stream.

exercises.title: Configure a no-loss, no-duplicate producer
exercises.task: Configure a producer so an accepted record is never lost and retries can't create duplicates, even across leader failovers. State which single setting enables idempotence and what it forces for acks and retries.
steps: Set enable.idempotence=true (the master switch for EOS-grade producing)
Confirm it requires acks=all, retries>0, and max.in.flight<=5
Keep min.insync.replicas=2 on an RF=3 topic for durability
Understand the guarantee: exactly-once *per partition* for producer retries

solution.label: Producer properties
solution.code:
enable.idempotence=true      # dedups producer retries via PID + sequence
acks=all                     # implied by idempotence; wait for all ISR
retries=2147483647           # implied; safe because dedup is on
max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5   # <=5 keeps ordering
# broker/topic side: replication.factor=3, min.insync.replicas=2

exercises.solutionNotes: enable.idempotence=true is the one switch: the broker tags each producer with a PID and per-partition sequence numbers, so a retried batch is deduplicated. It forces acks=all, positive retries, and max.in.flight ≤ 5. Combined with min.insync.replicas=2 on RF=3, an accepted write survives a broker loss and retries never duplicate.

exercises.title: At-least-once consumer with manual offset commits
exercises.task: Write the poll loop for a consumer that must never lose a record (at-least-once): disable auto-commit and commit offsets only after the records in a batch are fully processed. Explain why committing before processing would risk loss and why this design can produce duplicates.

given.label: Risky pattern to avoid
given.code: enable.auto.commit=true   // commits on a timer, possibly before processing

steps: Set enable.auto.commit=false
poll() a batch, process every record, THEN commitSync()
On rebalance/crash before commit, the batch is redelivered (at-least-once)
Make processing idempotent since duplicates are possible

solution.label: Poll loop

exercises.solutionNotes: Committing after processing guarantees at-least-once: if the app dies mid-batch, nothing was committed, so the batch replays. Committing before processing (or auto-commit firing on its timer) would advance the offset past unprocessed records — those are lost on a crash. The cost of at-least-once is possible duplicates, so downstream effects must be idempotent.

exercises.title: Exactly-once consume–transform–produce with transactions
exercises.task: An app reads from input, transforms, and produces to output. Achieve exactly-once end-to-end so that on failure neither a duplicate output nor a skipped input occurs. Wire up the transactional producer, and atomically commit the consumer offsets as part of the producer transaction.

given.label: Building blocks
given.code:
producer: transactional.id set
consumer: isolation.level=read_committed, enable.auto.commit=false

steps: Set a unique transactional.id and call initTransactions()
Per batch: beginTransaction(), produce outputs
sendOffsetsToTransaction(consumedOffsets, groupMetadata) — offsets join the txn
commitTransaction() (or abort on error); consumers read with read_committed

solution.label: Transaction loop

exercises.solutionNotes: Exactly-once for consume-transform-produce needs the output records and the input offsets to commit in one transaction. sendOffsetsToTransaction folds the consumer's progress into the producer's transaction, so either both happen or neither does. Downstream consumers must use isolation.level=read_committed so they never see aborted output. In Kafka Streams this is one line: processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2.

exercises.title: Avro + Schema Registry with a backward-compatible change
exercises.task: Produce Avro records for orders-value using Schema Registry, then evolve the schema by adding an optional field under BACKWARD compatibility so existing consumers keep working. Show how a consumer resolves the writer schema, and what change would be rejected.
steps: Configure the producer with the KafkaAvroSerializer + schema.registry.url
Evolve v2: add a field WITH a default (required for BACKWARD)
Register/verify compatibility; the serializer embeds the schema ID in each record
A consumer reads the schema ID from the record and fetches that exact writer schema

solution.label: v2 schema + why it's compatible

exercises.solutionNotes: Each Avro record carries a 5-byte header with the schema ID; the consumer fetches that writer schema (not "latest") and deserializes against it, then projects onto its reader schema. Under BACKWARD compatibility a new schema must be readable by consumers on the old one — so adding a field needs a default, and adding a required field (or removing one) is rejected. Run the compatibility check in CI to block breaking changes.

exercises.title: Build a word-count Streams topology
exercises.task: Write a Kafka Streams topology that reads lines from text-input, splits them into words, and continuously counts each word into word-counts. Identify which parts create an internal repartition topic and which create a changelog topic.

given.label: Topics
given.code:
input:  text-input  (key: null, value: line of text)
output: word-counts (key: word, value: running count)

steps: Stream text-input; flatMapValues to split into words
groupBy(word) — the re-key triggers a repartition topic
count() into a KTable — its state store is backed by a changelog topic
toStream().to("word-counts", Produced with Serdes.String/Long)

solution.label: Topology
solution.code:
StreamsBuilder b = new StreamsBuilder();
b.<String,String>stream("text-input")
 .flatMapValues(v -> Arrays.asList(v.toLowerCase().split("\\W+")))
 .groupBy((k, word) -> word)          // ⇒ repartition topic
 .count(Materialized.as("counts"))     // ⇒ changelog topic
 .toStream()
 .to("word-counts", Produced.with(Serdes.String(), Serdes.Long()));

exercises.solutionNotes: groupBy re-keys the stream, so Streams inserts an internal repartition topic to co-locate identical words on the same task. count() maintains a state store whose fault tolerance comes from an internal changelog topic — on restart the store is restored by replaying it. These internal topics are created and managed automatically under the application id.

exercises.title: Windowed aggregation with a single result per window
exercises.task: Count orders per customer in fixed 1-hour tumbling windows, but emit only the final result per window to the sink (not every intermediate update). Use a grace period for late events and suppress intermediates. Explain event-time vs the window close.

given.label: Requirement
given.code:
input: orders (key: customerId)
want: exactly one (customer, window) → finalCount, after the window closes

steps: groupByKey then windowedBy a 1-hour TimeWindows with a grace period
count() the windowed aggregation
suppress(untilWindowCloses(unbounded())) to emit only finals
Windowing is driven by event time (record timestamps), so late records within grace still count
exercises.solutionNotes: Tumbling windows are fixed, non-overlapping buckets keyed by event time.
