Without suppression a windowed aggregate emits an update on every record; suppress(untilWindowCloses) holds results until the window (plus its grace period) closes and emits one final value. The grace period is what lets a late-but-not-too-late record still be counted before the window is finalized.

exercises.title: Run a JDBC source connector with Avro and an SMT
exercises.task: On a distributed Connect cluster, create a JDBC source connector that streams a Postgres table into Kafka as Avro (schemas in Schema Registry), masks the ssn field, and routes records to a topic prefixed db_. Do it via the REST API and explain where the connector config and offsets are stored.

given.label: Cluster
given.code:
distributed Connect workers behind http://localhost:8083
Schema Registry at http://localhost:8081

steps: POST the connector config to /connectors (not a properties file — distributed uses REST)
Use AvroConverter + schema.registry.url for value conversion
Add a MaskField SMT for ssn and a RegexRouter (or topic.prefix) for db_ topics
Config, offsets, and status live in Connect's internal topics (replicated across workers)
exercises.solutionNotes: Distributed Connect is managed entirely through the REST API; there are no per-worker properties files for connectors. The cluster stores connector configs, offsets, and status in internal Kafka topics, so any worker can pick up a task if another dies. Converters (AvroConverter + Schema Registry) handle serialization, while SMTs (MaskField, RegexRouter) do lightweight per-record transformation in the pipeline.

exercises.title: Make a sink connector skip bad records into a DLQ
exercises.task: A sink connector keeps dying on the occasional malformed record. Reconfigure it to tolerate bad records, route them to a dead-letter queue topic with error context, and keep processing the good ones — without losing evidence of what failed.

given.label: Current behavior
given.code: one bad record → task FAILED → pipeline stops

steps: Set errors.tolerance=all so bad records don't kill the task
Set errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name to a DLQ topic
Enable errors.deadletterqueue.context.headers.enable=true for the failure reason
Keep errors.log.enable=true to also log the errors
exercises.solutionNotes: errors.tolerance=all lets the task skip records that fail conversion/transformation instead of dying. Pointing errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name at a DLQ preserves each bad record — and with context.headers.enable=true, the failure reason travels in the record headers so you can triage later. The good records keep flowing, so one poison record no longer halts the pipeline.

exercises.title: Test a Streams topology with TopologyTestDriver
exercises.task: Write a synchronous unit test for the word-count topology (ex-07) using TopologyTestDriver — no running broker. Pipe input records in, read output records out, and assert the counts. Note one class of risk this test can not cover.
steps: Build the Topology and wrap it in a TopologyTestDriver with test props
Create input/output test topics with the right serdes
pipeInput words, then readValue() from the output topic
Assert running counts; remember TTD can't catch real broker/serde/network issues

solution.label: Test

exercises.solutionNotes: TopologyTestDriver runs a topology in-memory and synchronously, so tests are fast and deterministic — ideal for asserting transformation and windowing logic. What it can't catch are integration risks: real broker behavior, serializer/Schema-Registry wiring, consumer rebalances, and end-to-end delivery. Those need an integration test against an embedded or real cluster.

exercises.title: Diagnose consumer lag from the CLI
exercises.task: A downstream team reports stale data. Use kafka-consumer-groups to read the group's current offset, log-end offset, and lag per partition, then interpret two patterns: lag growing on one partition only, versus lag growing on all partitions during business hours.
steps: Run --describe --group billing-app and read CURRENT-OFFSET, LOG-END-OFFSET, LAG
lag = log-end-offset − current-offset per partition
Lag on ONE partition → a hot key / skew or one stuck consumer/slow record
Lag on ALL partitions in business hours → the group is under-provisioned for peak

solution.label: Describe + read lag
solution.code:
kafka-consumer-groups --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --describe --group billing-app

# TOPIC     PARTITION  CURRENT-OFFSET  LOG-END-OFFSET  LAG  CONSUMER-ID
# payments  0          1000            1000            0    consumer-1
# payments  7          8400            50120           41720 consumer-3   ← hot
# ...
# lag = LOG-END-OFFSET - CURRENT-OFFSET

exercises.solutionNotes: Consumer lag is how far behind the head of the log a group is: log-end-offset − current-offset, per partition. Lag isolated to one partition points at data skew (a hot key) or a single stuck/slow consumer. Lag rising across all partitions at peak means the group can't keep up — add consumers (up to the partition count) or speed up processing. Lag is the number-one health signal for a consumer.
